courses.description: The course introduces students to major social and cultural issues in present-day Japan. It explores the important debates, problems, and challenges that have shaped Japanese society and culture in recent decades. The course encourages students to think critically about issues in Japanese society from a variety of disciplinary perspectives.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken JASP1020.
courses.learning_outcomes:
- state, recognize, recall and tell the main features of postwar Japan. Participants are expected to analyze the challenges faced by postwar and contemporary Japan and how they were surmounted successfully.

- recognize, describe and clarify the strengths and weaknesses, the advantages and disadvantages of the features and characteristics of contemporary Japan.

- compare and contrast Japanese developmental phases in the postwar years.

- apply, relate and deepen their appreciation of the significance of the dynamics and interaction of selected Japanese political, social, environmental, demographic developments. Deepen their appreciation of the significance of major developments within Japan. Deepen their appreciation of the significance of the dynamics of Japan’s domestic society, economy and politics; its interaction with its neighboring region and the rest of the world; examine Japan’s coping mechanisms in interacting with globalization and major recent world trends.

- analyze Japan's structure, policies, economy, society and current trends. Analyze the causes and effects of internal and external influences on Japan's development. Analyze the challenges that Japan faces, its ways of managing them and the resulting innovative features. Develop analytical frameworks for these analyses.

- generalize, reflect and hypothesize their own conclusions with regards to the course materials and contemporary issues

[Writing skills]

Through the graded short essay exercise required in the course, class participants develop skills in essay-writing and library research such as literature review and analytical skills. It is also an opportunity for participants to put their creative ideas in writing.

[Interactive skills]

Class participation and attendance facilitates interactions with other class participants, learning from each other and exploring alternative viewpoints. The graded tutorials enhances interactions with other class participants, learning from each other and exploring alternative viewpoints through oral presentations that include class discussions and debates. Subject to availability, guest lectures may also be possible as opportunities for class participants to interact with individuals with specific relevant expertise.

[Reading skills]

While lectures and tutorials are the main modes of knowledge exchange and learning activity, students are encouraged to critically examine the required readings and present their alternative viewpoints and comments in the tutorials.
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1: Background introduction (History)

Readings:

(1) Gordon, Andrew, "Global Power in a Polarized World: Japan in the 1980s" in A Modern History of Japan From Tokugawa Times to the Present (NY and Oxford: Oxford University Press), 2003, pp. 291-309 (18 Pages)

Tutorial:

The first tutorial will go through the requirements of the course. The first lecture will have an introduction of the course, how the participants are evaluated, the reading materials used

======================================================================

Week 2: An Era Beyond Recovery (History)

Readings:

(1) Gordon, Andrew, "Beyond the Postwar Era" in A Modern History of Japan From Tokugawa Times to the Present (NY and Oxford: Oxford University Press), 2003, pp. 310-332 (22 Pages)

Tutorial:

Class participants will work in groups to discuss their understanding of the readings and learn to adopt the perspective of their training, expertise and interest to analyze the readings, ask questions and discuss it in the context of their research interests.

=================================================================

Week 3: A Brave New Decade for Japan (History)

Readings:

(1) McClain, James L., "Chapter 17 Another New Century" in Japan: A Modern History, (NY and London: WW Norton and Company), 2002, pp. 599-632 (33 Pages)

Tutorial:

It plans to discuss early in the course broad strategies to tackle the short essay and build on essay writing skills learnt in other modules and courses, discussing thesis statement writing skills and question writing skills for the purpose of enhancing essay writing skills. Mid-term to be discussed, sample papers distributed for early reference. Discussion on research essay also includes items like thesis statement/hypothesis; important issues and research questions for inclusion in their essays and a table of contents. Opportunities are provided for class participants to ask questions on how to tackle the essay. Learn to cite sources and conduct library research.

===============================================================

Week 4: The Study of a Classic Popular Culture Icon in the age of Globalization (Popular Culture)

Readings:

(1) Shiraishi, Saya S., "Doraemon Goes Abroad" in Japan Pop!: Inside the world of Japanese popular culture (edited by Timothy J. Craig) (NY: East Gate), 2000, pp. 288-289

Tutorial:

Review readings in class covered up to this point of time in preparation for the mid-term. Participants get into groups to debate and take sides on crucial issues in the mandatory readings, in the process, identifying crucial issues and challenges in defending their viewpoints against contravening arguments. Discussions on what students think are possible mid-term exams if they designed the exam.

================================================================

Week 5: A Contemporary Japanese Popular Cultural Icon Captures the World (Popular Culture)

Readings:

(1) Allison, Anne, “Gotta Catch ‘Em All”: The Pokemonization of America (and the World)” and “Epilogue” in Millennial Monsters (Berkeley, LA and London: University of California Press), 2006, pp. 234-280. (46 pages) Divided into two lectures

Tutorial

A session to review readings in class covered up to this point of time in preparation for the mid-term. Participants get into groups to debate and take sides on crucial issues in the mandatory readings, in the process, identifying crucial issues and challenges in defending their viewpoints against contravening arguments.

==============================================================

Week 6: Pokemon in the age of Globalization (Popular Culture)

Readings: (Continued)

(1) Allison, Anne, “Gotta Catch ‘Em All”: The Pokemonization of America (and the World)” and “Epilogue” in Millennial Monsters (Berkeley, LA and London: University of California Press), 2006, pp. 234-280. (46 pages)

Tutorial:

Mid-term exam

===============================================================

Week 7: Robots in Popular Culture (Popular Culture and Technology)

Readings:

(1) Hornyak, Timothy N., "Chapter 6 Humanoids at home", in Loving the Machine the Art and Science of Japanese Robots (Tokyo, NY, London: Kodansha), 2006, pp. 85-100 (15 pages)

Tutorial:

Class participants get together in groups to go through a critical analysis of readings covered in the class up to this point of time, class participants will identify, categorize and compartmentalize major arguments while formulating a theme for their respective teams in preparation for class presentation. Sample final papers given out. Comparative exercises in class.

===============================================================

Week 8: Gender

Readings:

(1) Jackson, Keith and Miyuki Tomoioka, "Section 3: Constraints to Women's Career Choices" in The Changing Face of Japanese Management (London and NY: Routledge), 2004, pp. 103-112 (9 pages)

Tutorial:

Essay Hand-in and discussion of readings

=================================================================

Week 9: Japan in the World (Cultural Soft Power)

Readings:

(1) Tsutsui, William M., “Japan as Soft Superpower” in Japanese Popular Culture and Globalization (Ann Arbor: Association for Asian Studies Inc.), 2010, pp. 59-69.

Tutorial:

Short class presentation in groups. Class participants get together in groups to go through a critical analysis of readings covered in the class up to this point of time, class participants will identify, categorize and compartmentalize major arguments while formulating a theme for their respective teams in preparation for class presentation. Comparative exercises in class.

===============================================================

Week 10: Japan in the World (Environment)

Readings:

(1) Kirby, Peter, Wynn, "Chapter 7 Growth, Sex, Fertility and Decline" in Troubled Natures Waste, Environment, Japan (Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press), 2011, pp. 133-159 (26 pages)

(2) Morikawa, Jun, "Chapter 6 Conclusions", in Whaling in Japan Power, Politics and Diplomacy, (NY:Columbia University Press), pp. 119-129 (10 pages)

Tutorial:

Short class presentations in groups Class participants get together in groups to go through a critical analysis of readings covered in the class up to this point of time, class participants will identify, categorize and compartmentalize major arguments while formulating a theme for their respective teams in preparation for class presentation. Comparative exercises in class. Discussion of finals and discussion of readings

==============================================================

Week 11: Japan in the World (International Relations)

Readings:

(1) Koide, M, "A Japanese Perspective on the Rise of China and India: Opportunities, Concerns, and Potential Threats (M Koide)" in The Rise of China and India a New Asian Drama (Singapore: World Scientific), 2009.

Tutorial:

Short class presentation in groups. Discussion of finals

=======================================================================

Week 12: Japan in the World (Contemporary Japanese Society)

Readings:

(1) Screening of selected scenes from Lost in Translation with powerpoint presentation of social trends in Japan in the contemporary post-Bubble generation.

Tutorial:

Short class presentation in groups. Class participants get together in groups to go through a critical analysis of readings covered in the class up to this point of time, class participants will identify, categorize and compartmentalize major arguments while formulating a theme for their respective teams in preparation for class presentation. Sample final papers given out. Comparative exercise: make comparisons between Japan and other countries.

===============================================================

Week 13: Conclusion and Summary

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Japanese Culture and Society
courses.description:
(UGEC1232 is double-coded with JASP1100.)

The main objective of this course is to give students a general idea of Japanese cultural history and social background of modern Japan. Guidance leading to a better understanding of Japan will be provided.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for Japanese Studies Majors or students who have taken UGEC1232
courses.learning_outcomes:
1) Students are expected to gain an understanding of the main areas of Japanese life including work, family, gender roles, marriage, and religion. They are expected to understand the ways in which Japanese see their own society in terms of economic growth, Japan's place in the world, social order, and political structure.

2) Students are expected to understand and be able to use a variety of social theories and perspectives in understanding Japan. They are also expected to have gained an understanding of how these same perspectives might be used to analyze their own society, or other societies which they may wish to study in the future.

3) Students are expected to develop a clearer understanding of their own values regarding work, leisure, marriage, family, love, and religion through a comparison with the debates and competing interpretations of these values in Japan.

Remarks:

Department Board members disagreed with the suggestion that they replace the word 'understanding' with another word such as 'identity, explain, analyze or synthesize' as suggested by UGE.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Week 1: Images of** **Japan**

This lesson introduces students to widespread popular images of Japan in Asian and Western media and academia. It considers how and why these images have changed over time and asks students to consider the usefulness and limits of generalizations about other people and societies.

* *Week 2: Nation, Nationalism and the Imperial System**

This lesson introduces students to theories explaining nationalism. It then uses these theories to consider nationalism in Japan. It explores the Japanese Imperial system in the context of historical developments in Japanese nationalism.

* Read:*1)Akemi Nakamura. "Textbook Fight Not as Simple as it Seems" *The* *Japan* *Times.*7 August 2005.

2)Reiji Yoshida. "Panel Set to Ponder Female on the Throne." *The* *Japan* *Times.*25 January 2005.

* *Week 3: The "Economic Miracle"**

This lesson explores two main theories behind Japan's economic development and subsequent recession of the 1990s. It considers the reasons for Japan's economic success, the experience of this success for Japanese people, and asks students to consider competing interpretations of Japan's economic development.

* Read:* Kenneth B. Pyle, "Japan's Postwar National Purpose," chapter 3 in his *The-Japanese Question: Power and Purpose in a New Era*pp.20-41

* *Week 4: Law and Order**

This lesson examines changing perceptions of social order in Japan. It explores the catalysts behind these changing perceptions and considers the relationship between perceptions and reality. Students are asked to consider how Japan and their own societies maintain trust in the government, faith in social justice, and belief in the reliability of public order.

* Read:*1 )Wolfgang Herbert. "Yakuza and the Law" In Harumi Befu, Tom Gill, Jeffery Eades (eds.). *Globalization and Social Change in Contemporary* *Japan**.* Pp. 143-158.

2)Michael Williams. "Revolving Door: Many Japanese Banks Ran Amok While Led by Former Regulars. *Wall Street Journal (Eastern edition).* Jan 19, 1996.

* *Week 5: Work and Workplaces**

This lesson introduces competing interpretations of Japan's employment strategies. It urges students to consider the reasons behind these competing interpretations. It also helps students to consider the system from the perspective of Japanese workers according to gender, age, and educational level, and encourages them to consider their own strategies and values as future members of their society's workforce.

* Read:*1)Joy Hendry. "Careers and Continuity" in *Understanding Japanese Society* pp. 149-167

2)Yamada, A. "Death of a Salaryman" *Japan* *Quarterly.* Jul-Sep 1999 vol. 46 (3): 40-47

3)Mathews, Gordon. "Seeking a Career, Finding a Job: How Young People Enter and Resist the Japanese World of Work" In Gordon Mathews and Bruce White (eds.) *Japan* k *Changing Generations: Are Young People Creating A New Society?*

4)Yuko Ogasawara. "Gossip" in *Office Ladies and Salaried Men.*pp. 70 - 97.

* *Week 6: Education**

This lesson introduces five theories that explain the role of education in society. Students are asked to consider how these theories may be useful in understanding Japan's educational system and philosophies. Students are encouraged to consider their own beliefs and principles regarding the purpose of education and their strategies **as** students-within theu educational system.

* Read:*1)Joy Hendry. "The Education System" in *Understanding Japanese Socieiy pp.*96-114

2)Lee Cunin, Maria. 2004. "The Students Academic Experience: Students' Thoughts on Reform and the Future." In her *Student Views in* *Japan**: A Study of Japanese Students* ' *Perceptions of their First Years at University**.*** Pp. 119-138

* *Week 7: Home and Family**

This lesson introduces several theories regarding the family in modem society. It then explores changes in Japanese family forms over time. Students are asked to articulate their own values toward love, marriage, and family in contrast to what they are learning about Japan.

* Read:* 1) Joy Hendry. "The House and Family System" in *Understanding Japanese Society* pp.22-41

2) Anne Allison. "Family and Home" chapter 6 in *Nightwork: Sexualify, Pleasure and Corporate Masculinity in a* *Tokyo* *Hostess Club* pp. 102-113

* *Week 8: Women and Gender**

This lesson examines changes in women's and men's roles and ideals in Japanese society over time. Students are urged to reflect upon their own understandings of gender in comparison to perceptions and practices of gender common in Japan.

* Read:*1)Surniko Iwao. "Myths and Realities " in *The Japanese Woman: Traditional Image and Changing Reality.*pp. 1-30

2)Wiseman, Paul. USA *Today**.*** 'Wo Sex Please, We're Japanese." 2 June 2004.

* *Week 9: Leisure and Play**

This lesson explores the role of leisure in modem societies and the development of the concept and practices of leisure in modem Japan. Leisure in Japan is explored in terms of gender, age, class, and generational differences. Students are encouraged to articulate their own attitudes toward leisure in relation to work, to consider how perceptions of how leisure have changed over time in their society, and to discuss how they expect their own leisure practices will change asthey age.

* Read:* Anne Allison. "A Type of Routine." In *Nightwork.* P. *42-56.*

* *Week 10: Mass Culture and Popular Culture**

This lesson explores the development of mass culture in Japan and explains the ways in which popular culture may serve as the basis for both social change and the reproduction of the status quo. Students are asked to consider how their own participation in popular culture and mass culture both generates changes in their societies and reproduces global capitalist structures.

* Read:* Merry White. "The Marketing of Adolescence in Japan: Buying and Dreaming." In Lise Skov and Brian Moeran (eds.) *Women, Media and Consumption in* *Japan**. P.*255-273

* *Week 11: Religion**

This lesson explores basic religious traditions and philosophies in Japan and how the role of religion in Japan has changed over time. It considers the role of religion at the level of state politics as well as in everyday life. Theories regarding religion in modern society are introduced. Students are urged to develop their own interpretations of the role of religion in modern societies and in their own lives.

* Read:*1)Joy Hendry. "Religious Influences," *Understanding Japanese Society.* pp. 115-132

2)Pfeiffer, William Sanborn. "Makihari: New Religion and Japanese Popular Culture" *Journal of Popular Culture.* Fall 2000 vol. 34. Iss. 2, pp. 155 – 174

* *Week 12: Student Presentations**

* *Week 13: Conclusion**

This lesson reviews the social issues which Japanese consider to be most serious such as the emergence of diversity, subcultures, and changing youth expectations and desires regarding adulthood. It discusses, through surveys, how Japanese people view their society and how they view their future. It asks students to consider which problems are seen as the most critical in their societies, how their own societies view their future, and how popular views about their societies and about their societies' future are related to the beliefs held by individuals.

* Read:* (Read one-quarter of letters) "Rethinking the War Experience." In Frank Gibney. (ed.) *In SensG: The Japanese Remember the Pacific War Letters to the Editor of the Asahi Shimbun.* pp. 283-318.

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Understanding Japanese Languages and Culture
courses.description:
(UGEC1281 is double-coded with JASP1120.)

This course explores the relationship between Japanese language and culture. Everyday communication strategies adopted by people in Japanese society will be examined in terms of Japanese language expressions, styles and structures. Linguistic approaches will be the principal analytical tools employed in this course but interdisciplinary approaches will also be adopted. This course is designed for students who are interested in knowing more about Japan but who have very limited or no knowledge of the Japanese language. Student will gain an understanding of the basic structure of the Japanese language, learn how usage of the language reflects Japanese culture and behavior, and explore how communication patterns of Japanese speakers differ from speakers of other languages.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Students are expected to have gained a basic understanding of the structure of the Japanese language and historical changes of the writing system. Moreover, students will be able to understand the usage of the language, different styles and when each should be used, and different strategies used in expressing politeness.

2. Students are expected to understand the relationship between the language expressions and the behaviors of Japanese people in various circumstances and the reasons behind such behaviors.

3. Through comparing the culture of Japan with that of Hong Kong, students will become able to make comments on the characteristics of each and distinguish between the two.

4. Students will be able to perform an elementary study regarding Japanese culture and this will help the students to develop his/her further study in Japanese Studies.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Week 1**

* *Lesson 1**

Introduction to Japanese culture:

This lesson explains how Japanese culture influences daily life in Hong Kong today. The usage and the characteristics of Japanese language will also be discussed.

* *Week 2**

* *Lesson 2**

Introduction to the historical changes of kanji and kana in the Japanese language:

This lesson explains the historical changes of kanji and kana in Japanese language. Focus will be on why Japanese absorbed Chinese characters into their writing system and the process of how Chinese characters were converted to the modern *kana (hiragana and katakana)*.

* *Week 3 – Week 4**

* *Lesson 3**

Introduction to the phrases expressing linguistic politeness:

This lesson briefly explains the phrases expressing linguistic politeness. These include honorific expressions, expressions used in making requests, making apologies, expressing gratitude and personal opinions, giving advice and asking for permission. Emphasis will be placed on when and how these expressions are used. The relationship between such expressions and the characteristics of hierarchy in Japanese society will also be discussed.

* *Week 4 - 5**

* *Lesson 4**

Strategies used in making requests and refusals in Japanese:

This lesson discusses how Japanese make requests and refuse the requests of others. Such expressions are often used in daily life by Japanese but are not easily understood by foreigners. Special attention will be given to:

a. the difference in behaviour between Japanese and non Japanese in making requests and refusals.

b. how this behaviour reflects characteristics of Japanese culture.

* *Week 6 – Week 7**

* *Lesson 5**

Strategies used in making apologies and taking responsibility:

This lesson discusses how Japanese apologize and take responsibility after misconduct such as scandals or accidents. For example, we often see the top management of Japanese companies bow to the public to apologize for their misconduct and they may even quit the company in order to take responsibility for problems. In Hong Kong, it is rare to see such a phenomenon. We will discuss the mechanism of how Japanese make an apology and the effect of the Japanese way of apologizing upon social situations. Special attention will be given to the comparison of the styles of apology in Japanese and Hong Kong societies. We will also discuss the relationship between the Japanese way of apologizing and issues related to the Sino-Japanese War.

* *Week 8 – Week 9**

* *Lesson 6**

Ambiguity in the Javanese language:

Compared with other languages such as Chinese, there are many ambiguous linguistic practices in Japanese. These include, for example, the deletion of the subject "*watashi*" (I), the use of "*koto ni*

* narimashita*" (become something) rather than "*koto ni shimashita*" (I decided to do something) when talking about personal matters such as getting married, and the frequent use of intransitive verbs rather than transitive verbs. Most Japanese language learners including Hong Kong learners have experienced difficulties with these expressions and practices in their learning process. We will analyze and comment on this phenomenon.

* *Week 9 – Week 10**

* *Lesson 7**

The Business Manner of Japanese:

This lesson discusses how Japanese behave in business negotiations. Japanese are often criticized for not stating their true feelings and opinions. They often use expressions such as "*kangaesasetekudasai*" (Please let me think about it), "*kekkoo desu*" (It's O.K. /No, thank you) to indirectly express their opinions. We will discuss why Japanese behave in such a way and the correct way to deal with a Japanese business partner.

* *Week 10 - Week 11**

* *Lesson 8**

Japanese language in a sociolinguistic perspective:

In this lesson we discuss the following items of importance in sociolinguistics as they appear in the Japanese language.

a. the comparison between written and colloquial styles

b. the comparison of language usage for men and women

c. the comparison between the standard language and dialect

d. the comparison of language usage between the young and the elderly

e. the comparison between formal styles and informal styles

f. the comparison between jargon and standard expressions

* *Week 12**

* *Lesson 9**

The success and failure in learning Javanese language (with a focus on Hong Kong Cantonese speaking learners):

First, we will give a brief introduction to the theories of second language acquisition. Next, the factors affecting the results of the process of second language learning will be discussed. To ensure a better understanding, case studies comparing English language acquisition among Japanese and Hong Kong people will be introduced. Finally, by summarizing the above observations and the characteristics of both Japanese and Cantonese, we will comment on the difficulties experienced by Hong Kong Cantonese speaking Japanese learners.

* *Week 13**

* *Lesson 10**

Special topics on Japanese 1anguage and Japanese culture:

Special topics will be selected to help students understand the relationship between Japanese language and culture.

a. *"shin go ryuukoogo taishoo"* (New words and buzz terms Grand Prix)

b. *"kotoshi no kanji"* (The Chinese character of this year)

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Japanese Cinema

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 803
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 803

courses.description:
(UGEC1302 is double-coded with JASP1140.)

Film is regarded as an important medium for understanding culture and society. This course examines how representative works of Japanese cinema communicate fundamental aspects of Japanese culture and society as well as the human condition. Students will learn how this communication is made possible through the art of cinematic form (i.e., mise-en-scene, cinematography, editing, and sound), the common language shared among filmmakers around the world. The course introduces the basics of film analysis, giving students critical-thinking skills that will enable them both to see how Japanese cinema engages with world cinema and to understand how Japan—especially after the post-war—becomes a hub for myriad cultural flows.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken GESC2080, JASP1150 or UGEC1302.
courses.learning_outcomes:
| 1. **Identify** representative works of Japanese cinema. 2. **Evaluate** fundamental aspects about Japanese culture and society as represented in Japanese film. 3. **Consider** the relationships between Japanese film and world cinema through analysis of film language and cinematic technique. 4. **Develop** critical thinking and writing skills through discussion, classwork, essay writing, and a final project. |
| --- |
courses.course_syllabus:
Course format: Films will be made available for students to view outside of class as homework. Short film clips will be shown during class time for discussion and analysis. Each lesson begins with an introduction of the filmmaker and the film’s context in Japanese film history. Each lesson considers the manifold ways Japanese film engages with world cinema, in terms of form (i.e., mise-en-scene, cinematography, editing, sound) and/or content (e.g., theme, character, genre, etc.). In this way, students will learn how Japanese films can engage audiences in ways independent of their cultural and linguistic specificities.

* *Week 1**

* *Topic: Introduction to Japanese Cinema and Film Analysis**

- What is Japanese cinema? How do we read a film? What is film as an art form? How does film represent fundamental aspects of culture and society (such as literature, art, men, women, the environment, and food)?

* *Unit 1 | Literature on the Big Screen**

* In Japanese culture, cinema has been inspired—and continues to be inspired—by works of literature from Japan and around the world. This unit explores the deep influence of literature on Japanese cinema. Topics may include literary aesthetics (i.e., mono no aware), tropes from drama (i.e., Noh, Kabuki) and painting (i.e., ukiyo-e), poetic structure (i.e., renga), and adaptation of narrative (i.e., novels).*

* *Week 2**

* *Topic: Noh Theater and Atmospheric Grief**

Discussion and analysis

- Kore-eda Hirokazu, *Maborosi* (1995)

Reading:

- Donald Richie, “Kore-eda’s *Maborosi*: Showing Only What is Necessary”

* *Week 3**

* *Topic: Ukiyo-e and the Pathos of Things (*Mono no Aware*)**

Discussion and analysis:

- Mizoguchi Kenji, *Sansho the Bailiff* (1954)

Reading:

- Dudley Andrew, “Mizo Dayū”

- Mark Le Fanu, “*Sansho the Bailiff*: The Lessons of *Sansho*”

* *Unit 2 | Japanese Women**

* Women play an important role in Japanese culture and society. This unit explores representations of women in Japanese cinema—from the figural (i.e., monsters, supernatural beings) to the realistic (i.e., demimonde, housewife, mothers, sisters, office lady)—and how such representations raise questions about gender roles and the relationship between self and society.*

* *Week 4**

* *Topic: Woman and Landscape**

Discussion and analysis:

- Teshigahara Hiroshi, *Woman in the Dunes* (1964)

Reading:

- Audie Bock, “*Woman in the Dunes*: Shifting Sands”

- Linda Ehrlich and Antonio Santos, “The Taunt of the Gods: Reflections on *Woman in the Dunes*”

* *Week 5**

* *Topic: Working-Class Women**

Discussion and analysis:

Naruse Mikio, *When a Woman Ascends the Stairs* (1960)

Reading:

- Moeko Fujii, “Step by Step: Hideko Takamine in *When a Woman Ascends the Stairs*”

- Phillip Lopate, “*When a Woman Ascends the Stairs*: They Endure”

* *Unit 3 | Japanese Men**

* Men play an important role in Japanese culture and society. This unit explores representations of men in Japanese cinema—from the archetypal (i.e., ninja, samurai, yakuza) to the realistic (i.e., soldier, husband, father, salarymen)—and how such representations play within established film genres (i.e., Hollywood Western, film noir, and melodrama).*

* *Week 6**

* *Topic: Ronin as Rogue Cowboy**

Discussion and analysis:

Kurosawa, *Yojimbo* (1961)

Reading:

- Donald Richie, “Yojimbo”

- Alexander Sesonske, “*Yojimbo*: West Meets East”

* *Week 7**

* *Topic: Gangsters and Film Noir**

Discussion and analysis:

Shinoda Masashiro, *Pale Flower* (1964)

- Discussion and film analysis

Reading:

- Chuck Stephens, “*Pale Flower*: Loser Takes All”

* *Week 8**

* *Topic: Salarymen and Melodrama**

Discussion and analysis:

Ozu Yasujirō, *An Autumn Afternoon* (1962)

Reading:

- Geoff Andrew, “*An Autumn Afternoon*: A Fond Farewell”

* *Unit 4 | Japanese Environment**

* The environment plays an important role in Japanese culture and society. This unit explores representations of the environment in Japanese cinema—from figural and fantastic responses to nuclear disaster (i.e., Godzilla) in the postwar era, to documentary-style meditations on ecological crisis in the present day.*

* *Week 9**

* *Topic: Radioactive Monsters**

Discussion and analysis:

Honda Ishirō, *Godzilla* (1954)

Reading:

- J. Hoberman, “*Godzilla:* Poetry After the A-Bomb”

* *Week 10**

* *Topic: Water and Myths of Nature**

Discussion and analysis:

Hamaguchi Ryūsuke, *Evil Does Not Exist* (2023)

* *Unit 5 | Japanese Food**

* Food plays an important role in Japanese culture and society. This unit explores the representation of food in Japanese cinema, investigating how food mediates human connection within Japan and around the world.*

* *Week 11**

* *Topic: Ramen and Community**

Discussion and analysis:

Itami Jūzō, *Tampopo* (1984)

Reading:

- Willy Blackmore, “*Tampopo*: Ramen for the People”

- Linda Erhlich, “Community and Connection: Itami Jūzō’s *Tampopo* (1985)”

* *Week 12**

* *Topic: Japanese Home-Cooking Across the Globe**

Discussion and analysis:

Ogigami Naoko, *Kamome Diner* (2006)

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Japanese Popular Culture in Cultural Globalization
courses.description:
(UGEC1271 is double-coded with JASP1160.)

This course examines the development and academic significance of various forms of Japanese popular culture such as films, comics, music, TV dramas and culinary culture, from multidisciplinary perspectives, including history, cultural anthropology, cultural studies and media studies. It aims to deepen understanding the following three facets of Japanese popular culture:

1) the applicaton of cultural globalization theory in Japan; 2) the global strategy of Japanese cultural industries; and
3) social and intellectual transformation of contemporary Japanese society.

Using concrete examples and grounding on representative academic writings, this course discusses the globalization of Japanese popular culture and relevant issues in indigenization, glocalization, hybridization, transnational cultural flows and media-mix. Students are trained to understand the nature and cultural implications of Japanese popular culture in an Asian context. Besides lectures, discussions and readings, this course selectively utilizes topical essays (projects), e-learning, audio-visual materials and fieldwork-based assignments as supplementary teaching tools.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken JASP1110 or UGEC1271
courses.learning_outcomes:
Through a historical and cultural analysis of different facets of Japanese popular culture, this course aims to achieve the following three academic goals:

1)It aims to use Japanese popular culture as a medium or stepping stone to enhance students’ interest in and understanding of Japanese history, society and culture.

2)It aims to deepen understanding of nature and impact of Japanese cultural industry and its cultural implications in an Asian context.

3)It aims to discuss Japanese popular culture within the theoretical framework of cultural globalization. Having taken this course, students will be equipped with a basic ability to analyse cultural phenomenon in the lens of such cultural concepts as indigenization, glocalization, hybridization, transnational cultural flows, media mix, cultural colonialism and orientalism.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Main Objective****:**

This main purpose of course is to examine the global popularization and localization of Japanese popular culture from historical, cultural and comparative perspectives within the theoretical framework of cultural globalization. It aims to indicate that the cultural flows between Japan and Asia are by no means one-way traffic and Asia has been interacting and collaborating with the Japanese in creating, manufacturing, marketing and distributing cultural products for decades. Lectures, discussions and readings highlight different facets of cultural acculturation and transnational interaction found in Japan-Asia cross-cultural flows, such as appropriation, hybridization, global localization business strategy, the making of localized editions, the local application of Japanese formula, joint production, and the participation of foreign artists and staff. Solidly grounding on empirical data as well as theories from cultural globalization and cross-cultural studies, this course will be a pioneering teaching and learning project to explore uncharted research territories in Japanese studies, Japan-Hong Kong relations, and cultural studies.

* *Key Issues and Problems****:**

The following key issues and problems regarding the globalization of Japanese popular culture in an Asian context will be addressed in this course within the theoretical fiarnework of cultural globalization:

1) The idea that the influx of Japanese cultural products is a form of cultural imperialism that aims to expand Japanese economic interest and to spread Japanese value and lifestyle in Asia will be empirically and theoretically reexamined and challenged. It shows that cultural imperialism is an outdated model in cross-cultural studies, because it fails to understand the dynamic mechanism in transnational cultural flows and cultural forces of localization and hybridization.

2) While using "glocalization" (Roland Robertson uses it to refer to the localization strategy employed by multi-national companies to promote their products in different nations) to investigate the global business strategy of Japanese companies such as Sony, Nintendo, Capcom and Bandai in Asian and global markets, this course underscores the limitation of the glocalization theory in the East Asian context, as it fails to see the initiative taken by Asian artists and businessmen to creatively and selectively domesticate Japanese elements.

3) This course uses Japanese popular culture as a mirror or text to decode social and cultural issues of contemporary Japan. For examples, themes about ethnicity, gender, social hierarchy, political ideology, and cultural identity will be examined in the lens of Japanese popular culture.

* *Core Content, Structure and Selected Topics****:**

The course content of this course investigates the three facets of Japanese popular culture: (1) the application of cultural globalization theory in Japan, (2) the global strategy of Japanese cultural industries, (3) social and intellectual transformation of contemporary Japanese society. Accordingly, its structure follows the design of core content and contains the following three parts:

Part 1: Japanese Popular Culture and Cultural Globalization: Theories and Practices

Selected Topics:

(a) Introduction to Basic Theories in Cultural Studies and Cultural Globalization

(b) Globalization of Japanese Popular Culture in an Asian Context

(c) Reexamining the Cultural Imperialism, Orientalism and *Nihonjinron*Discourses

Selected Topics:

Selected Topics:

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Modern Sino-Japanese Relations
courses.description: Sino-Japanese relations are an important dimension of the history of modern East Asia. This course analyzes this multifaceted relationship from the mid-19th century to the present day. It will pay special attention to such topics as mutual perception, territorial and historical disputes, immigration, boycotts and collaboration, cultural diplomacy, postwar rapprochement, and attempts to move into a new age of Sino-Japanese coexistence. A main theme of discussion will be the need for accommodation and cooperation despite continual tension between the two countries.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. 德川時代在日之華人 (The Chinese in Tokugawa Japan)

本課探討德川時代日本華僑社會形成的背景、因素及過程，又考察長崎華僑融入日本社會，改變居住生活形態的發展，以及中國儒學對德川時期二元政治體制盛衰之影響。

2. 明治、大正時代在日之華人 (The Chinese in Meiji and Taisho Japan)

本課分析19世紀末即明治後期中國人留學日本潮流產生的背景、因素及具體情況，並探究大正時代留日潮流的反覆發展，以及留日學生的政治化趨勢。

3. 中日甲午戰爭 (1894-1895) (The First Sino-Japanese War 1894-1895)

本課分析甲午戰爭爆發的背景和始末、戰爭期間及以後清朝對日觀念的變化，以及戰後的中日關係
發展。

4. 在清朝民國之日本人 (The Japanese in Qing and Republican China)

本課探討甲午戰爭前日本人在華的情報活動和戰爭前後日本人在華經營的通信報刊活動。

5. 日本對華之文教政策 (The Japanese Cultural and Education Policy towards China)

本課考察近代日本在華文教活動的產生及發展、各種教育活動的內容，以及二次大戰期間日本在學校推行的同化教育。

6. 華人的反日與附日 (The Chinese Resistance and Collaboration with the Japanese)

本課一方面探討民初、五四運動時期中國教科書的反日思想、1928年五三慘案以後的反日運動，另一方面分析抗日戰爭期間附日勢力出現的原因，諸勢力的類型、活動以及日本招攬漢奸的政策。

7. 二戰後在日之華人I (The Chinese in Postwar Japan I)

本課探討二次大戰以後至今日本華僑的人數增長情況、中華街華僑的經濟事業發展，以及新一代華僑的經濟結構變化。

8. 二戰後在日之華人II (The Chinese in Postwar Japan II)

本課考察二次大戰以後至今日本新舊數代華僑的不同程度同化情況，又探討
戰後華僑學校的教育問題及華僑的婚姻觀念演變。

9. 駐華之日本外交官 (The Japanese Diplomat on China)

本課說明駐華日本國總領事館包括在香港日本國總領事館的主要角色，指出日本領事充當促進中、日溝通、深化兩國關係的橋樑，並以近來數任在香港日本國總領事的訪問和演講，揭示近來日本的對華政策。

10. 中日的相互理解 (Mutual Perception between the Chinese and the Japanese)

本課考察在近、現代各階段中、日兩國的相互認識和觀感的演變發展及其諸發展跟兩國政治、外交、經濟、軍事等方面變化的互動關係，並探討近年中日兩國致力構築相互認識的平台的趨勢。

11. 日本與中國威脅論 (Japan and the ‘China Threat’ Theory)

本課考察1980年代至今中國的急速經濟發展及經貿策略演變，並分析90年代以來東亞國家經貿分工結構的轉變、中國跟東亞諸國尤其日本的經貿關係發展，以及日本先後產生「中國威脅論」和「中國機會論」的輿論變化。

12. 中日糾紛 (Sino-Japanese Disputes)

本課分析1990年代以來影響中、日兩國關係的國際環境和日本國內環境的種種變化，以及損害兩國關係的若干因素，包括日本政治右傾化問題、兩國的歷史觀衝突、靖國神社參拜問題、新「日美防衛合作指針」及台灣問題等等。

13. 中日關係的前景 (The future of Sino-Japanese Relations)

本課探討中、日兩國在亞洲地區共同化解衝突，協作發展的前景，如共同維持東亞地區的和諧穩定，促進該區的經濟互惠合作、安全和平及災難處理等發展方向。

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Tourism and Sustainable Development
courses.description:
(UGEC1372 is double-coded with JASP1190.)
Since 2015, tourism has been listed by the United Nations as one of the means to achieve sustainable development goals. How does tourism contribute to the sustainable development of human society? What are the impacts of tourism upon the cities and communities? Using Japan as a principal example, this course aims at examining the possibilities and challenges of achieving sustainable development goals through tourism. The first part of this course introduces basic concepts of tourism and sustainable development goals, and discusses their relationship. The second part of the course compares Japan to China and other countries, and investigates the benefits and costs of tourism in relation to sustainable cities and communities, indigenous culture, and natural resources. Additional discussions further explore the potentials of sustainable tourism in Hong Kong. With assigned readings and coursework, students are expected to acquire knowledge on sustainable development goals, and critically evaluate tourism as a means to achieve these goals.
courses.course_syllabus:
Part One – Overview

Week 01: Overview: Why Do People Travel? How Does Traveling Relate to Sustainable Development Goals?

* This lecture topic introduces basic theories in social studies of tourism, and the current discussions on tourism in relation to sustainable development goals.

Week 02: Introduction to Sustainable Development Goals

* This lecture topic further examines the UN Sustainable Development Goals, and requests students to discuss the possible challenges of achieving these goals through tourism.

Week 03: The Sustainable Destination

* There is no tourism without destinations. This lecture topic examines features that make a destination sustainable.

Week 04: Overtourism? Cases of Kyoto, Macau and Venice

* Overtourism is one of the keywords that address the problems incurred by tourism. Drawing cases from both Europe and Asia, this lecture topic examines these problems, and invites students to suggest solutions.

Part Two – Sustainable Development and Tourism in Japan and Beyond

Week 05: Overview of Tourism in Japan

* This lecture topic introduces the socioeconomic background in the historical development of tourism in Japan. Drawing Japan as an example, students are encouraged to reflect on the social and political agenda of tourism.

Week 06: Gentrification: The Impacts of Urban Tourism

* This lecture topic examines the problem of urban gentrification and its relation to tourism in Japan, China and beyond.

Week 07: Mega Events

* Drawing on the Olympic Games as one of the examples, this lecture topic scrutinizes the effects and impacts of hosting mega events to attract tourists in Japan and beyond.

Week 08: Cultural Heritage and Local Communities

* This lecture topic scrutinizes the impacts of tourism on local communities at world cultural heritage sites.

Week 09: Environment and Ecotourism

* The lecture topic examines ecotourism and its impact on the local and global environment.

Week 10: Tourism and Indigenous Culture

* This lecture topic investigates the role and impact of tourism on the sustainability of indigenous culture.

Week 11: Tourism and Rural Revitalization

* This lecture topic focuses on the possibilities and challenges of introducing tourism for sustainable development in depopulating regions in Japan and beyond.

Week 12: Tourism and Disasters

* This lecture topic discusses the phenomenon of transforming places associated with tragedy into tourist attractions, its cultural meanings, and social impacts upon local communities in Japan and beyond.

Week 13: Discussion: Sustainable Development and Tourism in Hong Kong and Japan

* During the concluding lecture, students are invited to exchange their views on sustainable development in Hong Kong, in comparison to Japan and other localities discussed over the semester.

courses.required_readings:
Week 01: Overview: Why Do People Travel? How Does Traveling Relate to Sustainable Development Goals?

Urry, John, and Jonas Larsen. 2011. “Theories.” In *The Tourist Gaze 3.0*, 1-30. Los Angeles,Calif.; London: SAGE.

Week 02: Introduction to Sustainable Development Goals

Boluk, Karla A., Christina T. Cavaliere, and Freya Higgins-Desbiolles. 2019. “A Critical Framework for Interrogating the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals 2030 Agenda in Tourism.” *Journal of Sustainable Tourism* 27(7): 847-64.

Week 03: The Sustainable Destination

Fennell, David A., and Chris Cooper. 2020. “The Sustainable Destination.” In *Sustainable Tourism: Principles, Contexts and Practices*, 150-74. Bristol, UK; Blue Ridge Summit, PA: Channel View Publications.

Week 04: Overtourism? Cases of Kyoto, Macau and Venice

Visentin, Francesco, and Dario Bertocchi. 2019. “Venice: An Analysis of Tourism Excesses in an Overtourism Icon.” In *Overtourism: Excesses, Discontents and Measures in Travel and Tourism*, edited by Claudio Milano, Joseph M. Cheer and Marina Novelli, 18-38. Wallingford, Oxfordshire, UK; Boston, MA: CAB.

Week 05: Overview of Tourism in Japan

Week 07: Mega Events

Müller, Martin, Sven Daniel Wolfe, Christopher Gaffney, David Gogishvili, Miriam Hug, and Annick Leick. 2021. “An Evaluation of the Sustainability of the Olympic Games.” *Nature Sustainability* 4: 340-8.

Week 08: Cultural Heritage and Local Communities

Jimura, Takamitsu. 2011. “The Impact of World Heritage Site Designation on Local Communities – A Case Study of Ogimachi, Shirakawa-mura, Japan.” *Tourism Management* 32(2): 288-96.

Week 10: Tourism and Indigenous Culture

Lewallen, Ann-Elise. 2016. “Signifying Ainu Space: Reimagining Shiretoko’s Landscapes through Indigenous Ecotourism.” *Humanities* 5(3): 59.

Week 11: Tourism and Rural Revitalization

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Japanese History: From Samurai to Salaryman

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 401
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 401

courses.description: This is an introductory course to Japanese history from ancient to contemporary times. Beginning with the peopling of the Japanese archipelago, the course offers a sweeping coverage of the major historical transformations that Japan has passed through from its ancient to modern times. The first half of the course will explore the people, institutions, and ideas and processes that form what we know of “traditional” Japan. The second half of the course will deal with the modern era, where Japan underwent one of the most striking transformations in world history. In the process, students will learn about different approaches to the study of Japan and will use Japan as a window through which we can better understand the dilemmas of modernity that challenge us all.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Students will understand major transitions in Japanese history from ancient to medieval and modern times.
2. Students will learn how scholars and historians approach the study of Japanese history.
3. Students will learn history through reading a range of primary and secondary sources.
4. Students will be able to reflect on Japan to learn about transformations in global history.
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1: Introduction: Peopling the Archipelago

Week 2: The Early Japanese State

Week 3: The Capital of Peace and Tranquility

Week 4: The Rise of the Warrior Class

Week 5: Warrior Society

Week 6: Chaos and Reunification: Sengoku Japan and the Three Unifiers

Week 7: Tokugawa Japan

Week 8: Black Ships and Samurai

Week 9: Japan’s Modern Revolution I

Week 10: Japan’s Modern Revolution II

Week 11: Interwar Crisis and Renewal

Week 12: Imperial Japan at War

Week 13: Postwar Politics and the Economic Miracle

Week 14: Postwar Politics, Society, and Culture
courses.feedback_evaluation: Students will be asked to fill out a survey and a university course evaluation at the end of the semester.
courses.required_readings: This course will be taught in English or Mandarin, and the required readings will vary depending on the course teacher.
courses.recommended_readings:
Farris, William Wayne. *Japan to 1600: A Social and Economic History* (University of Hawaii Press, 2009).

Friday, Karl L., ed., *Japan Emerging: Premodern History to 1850* (Westview Press, 2012).

Gordon, Andrew. *A Modern History of Japan: From Ancient to Modern Times.* 4th edition (Oxford University Press, 2019).

Katsu Kokichi. *Musui’s Story: The Autobiography of a Tokugawa Samurai.* Translated by Teruko Craig (University of Arizona Press, 1988).

Keene, Donald., ed., *An Anthology of Japanese Literature: From the Earliest Era to the Mid-Nineteenth Century* (Grove Press, 1994)

Yellen, Jeremy A. *Japan at War, 1914-1952* (Routledge, 2025).

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Japanese Language Tasting
courses.description:
[This course is offered via asynchronous online mode. Students should register the course via KEEP, a CUHK eLearning platform where the course materials are uploaded.]

This course aims to provide a ‘tasting’ opportunity for students who have no history of Japanese language learning before to start their first Japanese language learning. The topics in this course will cover pronunciation of ‘Kana’, simple daily life communication topics to express their favorite food, interest/hobby, shopping, places they want to go/things they want to do in Japan, etc. In order to have a better understanding of Japanese society, relevant Japanese cultural topics will also be included in this course. After completing this course, students will have basic knowledge of Japanese ‘Kana’ and be able to communicate in simple Japanese language with Japanese people on topics introduced in this course. For students who opt for further study in Japanese language, this course will provide a good foundation for them to have a smooth transition to the upcoming elementary course. On the other hand, for students who do not opt for further study, this course will also provide simple basic language knowledge for daily conversation with Japanese people while visiting Japan in the future.

Students who have attended any one year exchange study programme in Japan and/or passed any level of Japanese Language Proficiency Test (JLPT) are NOT allowed to take this course.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for students who have taken JASP1450-1490,2000,2450-2490,3180,3200,3310-3390,3600,4180,4310,4330,4350-4390,4440,4450,4510,4520
From 2023-24, students can only earn up to 2 units of these courses: CLCP/C1001,1101,JASP1350,HKSL/SPAN/GERM/KORE/FREN1001
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Students can recognize the pronunciation of Japanese ‘Kana’.
2. Students can understand some basic vocabularies and grammar patterns.
3. Students can communicate verbally in simple Japanese with basic vocabularies and grammar patterns in some common situations.
courses.course_syllabus:
There are 7 modules (M1-M7) in this course.

M1 Introduction of pronunciation of Japanese ‘Kana’ and greeting

M2 My self-introduction & Japanese culture topic 1

M3 My favorite food & Japanese culture topic 2

M4 My interest and hobby & Japanese culture topic 3

M5 Shopping & Japanese culture topic 4

M6 Tell someone what you want to do & Japanese culture topic 5

M7 Tell someone where you want to go & Japanese culture topic 6

courses.description: This course provides basic training in the Japanese language. Emphasis is placed on sentence pattern practice and basic grammar. It also includes oral drills, simple compositions, dictation, translation and reading. This course starts from the writing system and basic pronunciation. Course textbooks, course exercise books, examination papers and other course materials are written in Chinese and Japanese. Students taking this course are expected to have prior knowledge in the Chinese language. Advisory: Not for Japanese Studies Majors.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. use verbs of state (iru, aru), directional verbs (kuru, iku, kaeru) , and activity verbs (taberu, nomu, and benkyoosuru).

2. count and use appropriate quantifiers.

3. use na-adjectives and i-adjectives, and be able to conjugate according to the given situation.

4. use past and present tense, and continuous and resultative aspect appropriately.

5. use purpose expressions, such as V+ni.

6. use direct and indirect speech

7. ask when one doesn’t know how to read and what to say (~nan to iimasuka ~doo omoimasuka)

8. use secondary aspect of ~te aru

9. use particles of wa, ga, ni, de, and o.

10. speak with correct pronunciation.
courses.course_syllabus:
JAS1450 and 1460 are consecutive one-semester courses designed to provide comprehensive training in reading, listening, speaking and writing in Japanese at the elementary level for elective students with an emphasis on elementary grammar.

Students are required to attend four hours of language lessons per week and are expected to spend two hours on homework each week. Language lessons are conducted in four languages: Japanese, English, Cantonese and Mandarin. Teachers use various kinds of conversational practices based on natural conversation emphasizing the use of the language in context.

The examination papers are written in Chinese and Japanese, and it contains task(s) of Chinese to Japanese translation. Depending on the situation, an English translation of the Chinese texts can be provided for reference. However, in case there is any inconsistency or conflict in the translation between the Chinese and English texts, the Chinese version shall prevail.

After completing the courses, students are expected to reach novice-middle level, and continue to enrol in the intermediate level language courses.
courses.feedback_evaluation:
1. One month survey

2. Course evaluation
courses.required_readings:
JASP 1450 Text Books and Homework

『日本語1』and『日本語 1 Workbook』written and published by Department of Japanese Studies, CUHK.

The course textbooks (including grammar explanations and translation sections) are written in Chinese and Japanese, and there are Chinese to Japanese translation questions in the exercise books used. The language used for classroom teaching varies from teacher to teacher. Students should refer to “Class Details” for information about medium of instruction for individual classes.

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: New Comprehensive Japanese I
courses.description: This course provides basic training in the Japanese language. Emphasis is placed on sentence pattern practice and basic grammar. It also includes oral drills, simple compositions, dictation, translation and reading. This course starts from the writing system and basic pronunciation. Advisory: Not for S7 entrants admitted in and before 2012. Advisory: For Japanese Studies Majors only.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. make sentences using “~wa~desu” pattern.

2. make sentences using verbs of existence.

3. make sentences using basic action verbs.

4. make sentences using movement verbs.

5. use i-adjectives .

6. make past, present tense sentences using verbs and adjectives.

7. speak with appropriate pronunciation, speed, pause and prominence.
courses.course_syllabus:
Topic Contents/fundamental concepts

L1 watashi wa chuubundaigaku no gakkusai desu. - “~wa~desu” construction

L2 are wa jisho desu. - Demonstrative pronouns of “kore, sore, are”

L3 koko wa nihon kenkyuugakka no jibushitsu desu. - Demonstrative pronouns of location, “koko, soko, asoko”

L4 chin-san no uchi no chikaku ni mise ga takusan arimasu. - Locative particle of “ni”

L5 toshokan ni dareka imasuka? - Verbs of existence, “arimasu, imasu”

L6 reizooko ni nani ka kudamono ga ari-masuka? - Use of “nani ka arimasu ka” vs “nani ga arimauka” and “nani ka imasuk ka” vs “nani ga imasuka”, and “dare ka imasu ka” vs “dare ga imasuka”

L7 boorupen o kudasai. - “~wo kudasai” construction”

L8 watashi no tanjoobi wa jyuu gatsu hatsuka desu. - Numbers and dates

L9 rantaotoo e iki-mashita. - Verbs of movement “~e ikimashita” - Verbs of movement “~e ikimashita”

L10 satoo-san wa itsumo kono shokudoo de hirugohan o tabe-masu. - Action verbs and a particle “o”

L11 kotoshi no fuyu wa atatakai desu. - “I”-adjectives

2. use past and present tense, and continuous/progressive and resultative aspect appropriately.

3. use secondary aspect of ~te aru

4. use na-adjectives and i-adjectives, and be able to conjugate according to the given situation.

5. use purpose expressions, such as V+ni.

courses.course_syllabus:
L12 shatin wa shidukana machi deshita. - “Na”-adjectives

L13 tanaka sensei wa chuubundaigaku he nihongo o oshie ni kimasu. - Particle which indicates purpose when s.o. moves from one place to another

L14 daigaku eki e iku densha wa nanbansen desuka? - Noun modified sentences

L15 goji ni okite, nani o shi-masuka? - Particle which indicates time “ni”

L16 minibasu ni notte iki-mashita. - Compound verbs of “notte-ikimasu”, “itte-kimasu”

L17 rin-san, haitte-kudasai. - “Vt-te kudasai” construction

L18 koo-san wa ima toshokan de nihongo no benkyoo o shite-i-masu. - Garland of “V-te-imasu”

L19 koko ni atta terebi ga nakunarimashita. - Sentences (past) which modify a noun

L20 koo-san wa akai sukaato o haite-imasu. - Verbs of wearing, “haku, kiru, kaburu”

L21 mado kara maaonsan ga miemasu. - “~ga miemasu” construction

L22 kono ji wa nihongo de nanto yomimasuka? - “nan to yomimasu ka” “nan to iimasu ka” constructions

L23 hokano hito mo muzukashii to itte-imashita. - “to it-te imashita” construction

L24 nakamura-san no heya wa kireini sooji shite-arimasu. - “V-te arimasu” construction

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Japanese Communication Skills for Beginners 1
courses.description: This course aims to provide students with the opportunity to develop basic interactive skills in Japanese. Through interactive involvement in class activities, students will develop basic linguistic skills and socio-cultural competencies involved in interacting with Japanese speakers in everyday social situations in Japan and Japan-related contexts in Hong Kong. The course is designed for students with only very limited or no prior learning experience in Japanese. The medium of instruction is English and course materials are written in English and Japanese.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon successful completion of this course students will be able to:

1. Understand and use simple words and phrases in common everyday contexts.
2. Ask grammatically structured questions related to basic needs and respond using short phrases and sentences.
3. Communicate with native and non-native speakers of Japanese on basic daily life topics related to Japanese social contexts and Japan-related situations in a simple manner.
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1: Basics of Japanese pronunciation and writing system.

Hiragana and katakana.

Week 2: ‘*hajimemashite*’ How do you do?

“*Minna no Nihongo*” Elementary Japanese I, Lesson 1

N1はN2です。N1はN2じゃありません。N1はN2ですか。

Week 3: ‘*korekara osewa ni narimasu’* Thank you in advance for your kindness.

“*Minna no Nihongo*” Elementary Japanese I, Lesson 2

これ　それ　あれ　このN　そのN　あのN

Week 4: ‘*korewo kudasai’* I’ll have it, please.

“*Minna no Nihongo*” Elementary Japanese I, Lesson 3

NはPlaceです。ここ　そこ　あそこ　こちら　そちら　あちら

Week 5: *‘sochira wa nanji made desuka’* What are your opening hours?

“*Minna no Nihongo*” Elementary Japanese I, Lesson 4

Vます。Vません。Vました。Vませんでした。

Week 6: ‘*kono densha wa koushien he ikimasuka’* Does this train go to Koshien?

“*Minna no Nihongo*” Elementary Japanese I, Lesson 5

N (place)へ行きます。来ます。帰ります。どこへも行きません。

Week 7: ‘*issho ni ikimasenka’* Shall we go together?

“*Minna no Nihongo*” Elementary Japanese I, Lesson 6

NをV (transitive)　Nをします。何をしますか。なんandなに

Week 8: ‘*irasshai’* Welcome

“*Minna no Nihongo*” Elementary Japanese I, Lesson 7

N (tool/means)でV　N1 (person) にN2をあげます/もらいます。

Week 9: ‘sorosoro sitsurei shimasu*’* It’s time to go.

“*Minna no Nihongo*” Elementary Japanese I, Lesson 8

Adjectives　N1はどうですか。N1はどんなN2ですか。

Week 10: ‘*zannendesuga’* It’s a pity that…

“*Minna no Nihongo*” Elementary Japanese I, Lesson 9

Nがあります。Nが好きです。Nが嫌いです。上手・下手です。

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Contemporary Japan

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 405
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 405
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 405

courses.description: This course interrogates the major institutions, ideas and historical forces that shape life in contemporary Japan, and pushes students to examine the similarities and differences in processes of modernization across societies. As students have covered the historical and sociopolitical groundings of modern and contemporary Japan, this course goes deeper into the social science of Japan by examining the major social institutions of home, school, and work to facilitate our understandings of the primary forces of socialization and organization and the dynamics of power in contemporary Japanese social life. Furthermore, it addresses the new challenges emerging within each of these spheres based on Japan’s changing socio-economic and demographic landscapes. The study of Japan challenges us to reevaluate the premises of Western social theory as well as HK-centric conceptualization, and it rewards us with fresh understandings of the transformations to modernity and the nature and direction of modern life. Advisory: For Japanese Studies Majors only.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Students will be able to gain a broad grasp of how Japanese society as well as its broader issues are approached and analyzed.
2. Students will also be able to articulate thoughtful and well-founded opinions based on their understanding of course materials.
3. In addition, students will develop critical thinking skills, construct analytical essays, give PowerPoint presentations, and lead academic discussions.

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Critical Analysis in Japanese Studies

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 713
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 713

courses.description: The course provides students with the opportunity to analyze primary and secondary sources on Japanese history, society, and culture. Students will be introduced to methods of analysis and exposed to various types of primary sources. Students will write a research paper and present findings on a specialized topic using primary data and will learn to identify, use, and analyze appropriate academic sources in relation to the chosen topic. Teamwork and individual work will be required.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon successful completion of the course, students will be able to:

* analyze different kinds of primary sources
* assess and articulate the significance of primary sources in social and historical contexts and at national, regional, or global levels as appropriate
* identify and analyze academic sources written in relation to particular social issues
* use effective oral, written, and visual communication skills to make a sustained argument about a topic of interest about Japan.
* collaborate effectively as part of a team in professional and inter-disciplinary contexts

courses.title: Field Research Methods

courses.description: This course aims to provide students with an understanding of basic research methods for independent field research. Research methods such as interviewing, questionnaires, participant observation, and media surveys will be introduced and the merits of each will be discussed. The course will teach students how to design a research project and write a research proposal. Advisory: For Japanese Studies Majors only.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Students will gain an understanding of the merits and limitations of a variety of qualitative research methods.
2. Students will be able apply selected social science methodology to design their own research project in Japan.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction to the Course; Culture and Ethnography

Tutorial: McCurdy. “Culture and Ethnography” (Chapter 1) and “Microcultures” (Chapter 2) in *The Cultural Experience: Ethnography in a Complex Society.*

2. Getting Started: Research Ethics and Informants

Tutorial: Read: (1) McCurdy, “Cultural Informants” (Chapter 3) in *The Cultural Experience: Ethnography in a Complex Society.* (2) Fetterman, David. “Walking Softly through the Wilderness: Ethics” in his *Ethnography: Step by Step*. Pp. 136-146. (You are not required to read pages 129-135)

3 Participant Observation (Part 1)

Tutorial: Read: Tierney, Gerry. “Becoming a Participant Observer.” In Michael V. Angrosino. *Doing Cultural Anthropology*.

4. Participant Observation (Part 2) (Research Plan due)

Tutorial: Read: DeWalt and DeWalt. “Doing Participant Observation: Becoming a Participant” (Chapter 3) in *Participant Observation*. pp.35-66 (You may skip the section on “Participating and Parenting” bottom of page 61 to bottom of page 64)

5. Interviewing

Tutorial: Read: DeWalt and Dewalt. “Informal Interviewing in Participant Observation” (Chapter 7) in *Participant Observation*. Pp. 120-140.

6. Sampling and Questionnaires

Tutorial: Read: Russell, Bernard. 2011. “Questionnaire and Survey Research” in *Research Methods in Anthropology*. Pp. 187-197; pp. 201-207. Electronic Resource (There are several editions of this book. This assignment is from the online version.)

7. Writing Fieldnotes (Interview Questions due)

Tutorial: Read: DeWalt and DeWalt. “Writing Field Notes” (Chapter 8) in *Participant Observation*. pp.67-82.

8. Expanding the Research

Tutorial: Read: McCurdy. “Discovering Folk Terms” (Chapter 4) and

“Discovering Taxonomic Structure” (Chapter 5) in *The Cultural Experience: Ethnography in a Complex Society.*

9. Unobtrusive Observations

Tutorial: Read: 1) Price, Laurie. “Carrying out a Structured Observation” in Michael V. Angrosino. *Doing Cultural Anthropology*.

Recommended: DeWalt and DeWalt. “Becoming an Observer” (Chapter 4) in *Participant Observation*. pp.67-82.

10. Qualitative Data Analysis (Self-administered Questionnaire due)

Tutorial: Read: McCurdy: “Discovering Meaning” (Chapter 6) and “Ethnographic Detail and Cultural Focus.” (Chapter 7) in *The Cultural Experience: Ethnography in a Complex Society.*

Recommended: Hammersley, Martin and Atkinson. “Documents” in *Ethnography: Principles in Practice*

11. Analysis

Tutorial: Read: McCurdy. “Cultural Themes and Cultural Adaptation” (Chapter 8) and “Writing and Using Ethnography” (Chapter 9) in *The Cultural Experience: Ethnography in a Complex Society.*

12. Writing a Research Proposal

Tutorial: Read:

1) Pender, Jordan. “Ethnography of a Legal Secretary” in McCurdy et al. (eds) *The Cultural Experience: Ethnography in a Complex Society.*

2) Boehlke, Jennifer. “Juicing their Way to the Top: Ethnography of a Tattoo Shop” in McCurdy et.al. (eds.) *The Cultural Experience: Ethnography in a Complex Society.*

Recommended: DeWalt and DeWalt. “Analyzing Field Notes” (Chapter 9) in their *Participant Observation*. Pp. 163-194.

13. Student Presentations
courses.feedback_evaluation: Students are welcome to speak to the instructor about their views of the course. In addition, students will be asked to fill out a mid-term survey after about four weeks into the semester and a University course evaluation at the end of the semester.
courses.required_readings:
Angrosino, Michael V. 2002. *Doing Cultural Anthropology: Projects for Ethnographic Data Collection*. Prospect Heights, IL: Waveland Press.

DeWalt, Kathleen M. and Billie J. DeWalt. 2002. *Participant Observation: A Guide for Fieldworkers*. Walnut Creek, CA: AltaMira Press.

Fetterman, David M. 1998. *Ethnography: Step by Step*. Thousand Oaks, CA: Sage Publications.

Hammersley, M. and P. Atkinson. 2007. *Ethnography: Principles in Practice*. Second edition. London: Routledge.

McCurdy, David W., James P. Spradley and Dianna J. Shandy. 2005. *The Cultural Experience: Ethnography in a Complex Society.* Long Grove, IL: Waveland Press.

Russell, Bernard. 1994. *Research Methods in Anthropology*. Thousand Oaks, CA: Sage Publications.

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Japan and Asian Creative Industries
courses.description: This course examines the rise of Japan and Korea as leaders in global and regional creative and cultural industries from historical, sociological, ethnological and cultural perspectives. Lectures and tutorials cover areas in on-line games, idol-making, creative design, mobile phone culture, toys, film collaborations, etc. Lectures are theme-oriented with an aim to explore the role of the government and the private sectors in developing creative industries and the lesson for Hong Kong to learn from Japanese and Korean experiences.
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of this course, students will be able to:

1. Enhance the understanding of Japan and Asia’s cultural industries; Gain updated information from experts in the industry.
2. Analysis cultural phenomenon in Asia’s Cultural and Creational Industries systematically with independent thoughts.
3. Discover concrete directions of development of HK’s Cultural and Creational Industries.

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Introduction to Japanese Animation, Comic and Game
courses.description: Animation, comic and game (ACG) are perhaps the most globalized parts of Japanese popular culture. They have a very strong impact on global cultural industry and consumer culture, used by the Japanese companies to expand their economic activities overseas and by the Japanese government for cultural politics. This course examines issues relating to Japanese animation, comic and game from historical, sociological and comparative perspectives. In addition to lectures, this course promotes interactive teaching and learning by organizing group discussion, video session and panel discussion.
courses.course_syllabus:
PART 1: Introduction and Overview
Lecture 1: Introduction(1): What is ACG?
Lecture 2 & 3: The development of Animation Industry in Japan via “The Big Three Publisher”
PART 2: Animation Research
Lecture 4 & 5: Science Fiction Anime: Japanese “Five Kings”
Lecture 6: Otaku (1): Being the Bridge, or being abandoned?
Lecture 7: Otaku (2): Moe and Otaku
PART 3: Regional Comparative Study
Lecture 8: Game Industry : The development path between Japan and USA
PART 4: Regional Comparative Study
Lecture 9: ACG and Reality : 2.5 dimensions
Lecture 10: Sex and Gender (1) : The difference demand between two sexes for animation and games
Lecture 11: Sex and Gender (2) : Relationship between pornography and the development of Japanese ACG Industry
Lecture 12 :Review and prepare for the final exam
courses.feedback_evaluation:
One month student feedback： One month after the course started
End of course feedback： Usually three quarters through the course determined by department admin

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Culture of Japanese Mass Media
courses.description: This course discusses the cultural and social implications of mass media in contemporary Japan. Media contexts in Japan are not only regarded as a business or an entertainment but also an ideology, or a remarkable source of soft power in the world's different media capitals. This course focuses on the construction of the Japanese mass media context in the age of internet and globalization, investigating how the media contexts are being produced and manipulated and how the influence is being demonstrated.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Students are able to understand the game of Japanese media, formation of Japanese image, ideology and illusion, global influence from Japanese media;
2. Students can analyze how the Japanese media influences our daily life
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction: What is media? Why do we need to study Japanese media in a specific nature? What Media studies students supposed to do?

a. Lecture style: Trial and error Vs Sit and listen

b. What is the point of studying media?

c. What should we look into?

d. Who are the players?

e. Tips on Media Studies in the Internet age

* *Part A: Basic background discussions and theoretical training**

2. Media, desire and image of Japan (Part 1)

a. Media constructs reality, do you think so?

i. What’s in media? Please define. What do you think about HK: http:// www.bilibili.com/video/av1272268/

ii. What do you think about HK: https://www.youtube.com/watch? time\_continue=194&v=\_ediOgTBIFw

iii. How do you consume media?

iv. What are the concerned points, discussions and theories in media studies?

b. Neil Postman, Theodor Adorno...Tokit... Audiences are potatoes?

c. But do we really think when we watch TV (or other media context?)

d. Desire→images of Japan

e. Importance ... Question on Media Gisho

f. e.g. maji uta: http://v.youku.com/v\_show/id\_XNDA1ODc3MDg4.html

g. london hearts: http://tv.sohu.com/20111219/n329538499.shtml

h. After earthquake: http://v.youku.com/v\_show/id\_XNDY3MTEyNDA4.html

i. children in the school after earthquake: http://v.youku.com/v\_show/ id\_XNDQzMTU3ODgw.html

3. Media, desire and image of Japan (Part 2)

a. What’s the meaning of complaints in media?

b. Franjar-ology? — complaints in the media do really work?

c. Japanese situation and the other side of the world

d. Jamie’s School dinner -- is he complaining the correct point? http:// www.youtube.com/watch?v=t5wyXMchQ4c

e. Right complaints, wrong complaints and displaced complaints

4. Magazines and what’s inside? Anyone still reads Magazines?

a. Male magazine and desire: http://appnews.fanswong.com/show.php? id=347737&TORO

b. Female magazine and consumerism

c. GREAT points for media study students...

5. Mid-term report: Watch a video and answer a question

a. Deadline: , 2019.

b. Number of words: under 1000 words

c. Language: Chinese or English

* *Part B: Case Studies: how we apply the theories and context in reality?**

6. Game Show: What do we expect from game shows?

a. How can we construct a game show? Game show: what kind of games we see?

b. http://v.youku.com/v\_show/id\_XMjM1NzUwMDcy.html

c. How can a game be interesting? Try and error

d. GREAT points for media study students...

7. Food porn: why do we need to watch so many gourmet programme?

a. How often do you see food shots in TV?

b. Why people want to know “information” on food?

c. Techniques of food porn: how to make a good “food” programme?

d. Ramen densetsu: http://v.youku.com/v\_show/id\_XMjEwNTI0NDM2.html

e. Gourmet King: http://v.youku.com/v\_show/id\_XMjA3OTMyODAw.html

f. Docchi no ryouri show: http://v.youku.com/v\_show/id\_XNTEzODI0Nzgw.html

g. the best donburi: http://v.youku.com/v\_show/id\_XMjExMjEzODQ4.html

8. Invention of TRADITION and POPULAR

a. Traditional →what we see in traditional culture?

b. Kimono, food fusion and others: are we remaking traditions or ruining them?

c. Mobile games and studies: what kind of theories can we apply? Case study of Puzzle and Dragon.

d. Make a big hit is important?

9. What is suitable for children (generally)?

a. Cartoon and children’s programme: is that really suitable?

b. Denou→What is conveyed inside the programme?

c. What are the RULES of children programme?

10. What is inside Doraemon? What is suitable for children (in Japanese context) (1) ?

a. Close investigation of children texts

b. Are we really enjoying clean cartoons?

c. Is “indecent” information necessarily “bad” information?

d. What is important in children media texts in Japan?

11. Internet campaign and mixture of media (by Japanese)?

a. Close investigation of recent internet campaigns in Japan (Den-o the movie)

b. Case studies of Niconico, Funnashi and Kumamon

c. How to use Internet to produce new campaigns in Japan?

d. How to do VIRAL messages?

12. Tear Porn and Kandou industry

a. Why will you cry?

b. What will you do in order to make yourself cry?

c. Crying means something or not?

d. Tear as a buffer, product, life experience and lie. e.

13. Conclusion? Can we have any conclusion?
courses.feedback_evaluation: One-month mid-term survey and CTE will be conducted.

courses.description: This course is the continuation of JASP1460. Course textbooks, course exercise books, examination papers and other course materials are written in Chinese and Japanese. Students taking this course are expected to have prior knowledge in the Chinese language. Students are required to pass a placement test or obtain equivalent competence in Japanese before course registration.Advisory: Not for Japanese Studies Majors.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1) make complex sentences including time, quotations, conditionals, noun modifier, etc.

2) use basic functional phrases including purpose, cause, comparison, inference, etc.

3) use a variety of functional phrases including viewpoint, evaluation, hypothetical conditions, correspondence, tendency,

assertion, etc.

4) write simple texts following given structures and contents.

5) scan key words and skim over simple reading materials.

6) speak with correct pronunciation
courses.course_syllabus:
JAS2450 and 2460 are consecutive one-semester courses designed to provide comprehensive training in reading, listening, speaking and writing in Japanese at the intermediate level for elective students with an emphasis on both comprehension and expression.

Students are required to attend four hours of language lessons per week and are expected to spend two hours on homework each week. Language lessons are conducted in four languages: Japanese, English, Cantonese and Mandarin. Teachers use various kinds of conversational practices based on natural conversation emphasizing the use of the language in context.

The examination papers are written in Chinese and Japanese, and it contains task(s) of Chinese to Japanese translation. Depending on the situation, an English translation of the Chinese texts can be provided for reference. However, in case there is any inconsistency or conflict in the translation between the Chinese and English texts, the Chinese version shall prevail.

After completing the courses, students are expected to reach the intermediate-low level of Japanese language ability.

1. One month survey

2. Course evaluation
