The language used for classroom teaching varies from teacher to teacher. Students should refer to “Class Details” for information about medium of instruction for individual classes.

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Intimacy in Contemporary Japan

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 703

courses.description: How do people love, care, and desire in a changing world? Japan offers a rich terrain to explore this question, where shifting gender roles, evolving family forms, and diverse relational practices have long shaped public discussions about what counts as a “good” life and who gets to live it. Taking a feminist approach to intimacy as a world-making force that organizes life itself, this course examines how everyday relationships open onto the broader politics of living together. Throughout the semester, students will engage with feminist and queer theories to explore the themes of gender, sexuality, and family through various topics—from divorce and dating to hostess clubs, fandom love, self-marriage, pet families, and robotic companions. The course invites students to think about what worlds—of labor, affect, and belonging—intimacy makes possible, and how people inhabit them in Japan and beyond.
courses.learning_outcomes: By the end of this course, students will gain substantive knowledge of how gender, sexuality, and family shape the worlds of love, care, and desire in contemporary Japan. They will engage key social theories to analyze intimacy as a political force that organizes everyday life—its labor, affect, and belonging. Through close reading, discussion, and comparative reflection, students will develop critical skills to interpret cultural texts, connect ethnographic materials to theory, and articulate their own perspectives on the politics of living together in Japan and beyond.
courses.course_syllabus:
The course is organized into three thematic parts that move from the architecture of everyday life to the performance of desire and the politics of togetherness. Each week combines a lecture and a tutorial session: lectures provide historical and theoretical grounding, while tutorials invite close reading, discussion, and collaborative analysis of key texts and examples. Throughout the semester, students will engage with ethnographies, films, and theoretical writings to trace how intimacy works as a world-making force that shapes labor, emotion, and belonging in Japan. Two tutorial sessions will be reserved for review, group presentations, or other activities.

Part I. Home and Work: The Architecture of Gender/Sexuality in Everyday Life

Explores how the post-WWII ideals of family structured everyday life and how these arrangements are reconfigured in response to economic precarity and demographic change.

Themes: feminist political economy, care work, the family-state nexus.

Part II. Desire and Fantasy: The Performance and Discipline of Masculinity/Femininity

Examines gender/sexuality as performance and discipline through the worlds of hosts, hostesses, and media fantasies, linking emotion, sexuality, and labor.

Themes: performativity, affective labor, commodified sexuality.

Part III. Love and Kinship: The Politics and Ethics of Togetherness

Considers how new forms of love and kinship—fandom, pet families, solo marriage, and robotic companionship—redefine what it means to care for each other and live together.

Themes: intimate publics, (non)human companionship, relational ethics.
courses.feedback_evaluation: mid-term survey and course evaluation
courses.required_readings:
* Allison, Anne. 2000. *Permitted and Prohibited Desires: Mothers, Comics, and Censorship in Japan*. California, CA: University of California Press.
* Galbraith, Patrick W. 2019. *Otaku and the Struggle for Imagination in Japan*. Durham: Duke University Press.
* Shakuto, Shiori. 2025. *After Work: Japanese Silver Backpackers in Malaysia*. University of Pennsylvania Press.
* Takeyama, Akiko. 2016. *Staged Seduction: Selling Dreams in a Tokyo Host Club*. Stanford, CA: Stanford University Press.
* Ronald, Richard, and Allison Alexy, eds. 2011. *Home and Family in Japan: Continuity and Transformation*. Milton Park, Abingdon, Oxon; New York, NY: Routledge.

Excerpts will be provided by the instructor.
courses.recommended_readings: Lectures will include select theoretical writings on representation, performance, political economy, affect/emotion, fantasy/desire, and care ethics (e.g., Stuart Hall, Michel Foucault, Sigmund Freud, Joan Tronto, Nancy Fraser, Karl Marx, Judith Butler, Sarah Ahmed, José Esteban Muñoz, Adrienne Rich)

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Japanese Management

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 405
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 405
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 405
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 405

courses.description: This course introduces the historical and social development of present Japanese management practices and systems. It aims to equip students with better understanding of Japanese business people, to establish mutual understanding, and to help students learn to collaborate with staff from Japan so as to achieve common objectives.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Learning outcome: After completing this course, students should be able to:

- state, recognize, recall and tell the main features of Japanese management

- describe and clarify the strengths and weaknesses, the advantages and disadvantages of these features

- compare and contrast evolving features of Japanese management. Compare and contrast Japanese management perspectives between its different chronological phases as well as with other forms of management techniques.

- apply, relate and deepen their appreciation of the significance of the dynamics external and internal changes to Japanese management. Deepen their appreciation of the significance of Japanese management's adaptations and evolution according to chronological phases in its development.

- analyze Japan's management practices through case studies. Analyze changing economic needs, demographic shifts and evolving trends/lifestyles/habits in Japan

- generalize, reflect and hypothesize their own conclusions with regards to the course materials and contemporary issues

[Writing skills]

Through the graded short essay exercise required in the course, class participants develop skills in essay-writing and library research such as literature review and analytical skills. It is also an opportunity for participants to put their creative ideas in writing.

[Interactive skills]

Class participation and attendance facilitates interactions with other class participants, learning from each other and exploring alternative viewpoints. The graded tutorials enhance interactions with other class participants, learning from each other and exploring alternative viewpoints through oral presentations that include class discussions and debates. Subject to availability, guest lectures may also be possible as opportunities for class participants to interact with individuals with specific relevant expertise.

[Test-taking skills]

Class participants are required to take the mid-term quiz to gauge their ability to keep up with materials midway through the course, test-taking skills and an opportunity for class participants to test their own knowledge. The final exam evaluates class participants¡¦ overall understanding of the course materials and performance in the second half of the course.

[Reading skills]

While lectures and tutorials are the main modes of knowledge exchange and learning activity, students are encouraged to critically examine the required readings and present their alternative viewpoints and comments in the tutorials.
courses.course_syllabus:
Course Syllabus

Course Description English:

Japanese management is characterized by a combination of indigenous cultural influences, early postwar borrowing of ideas and creative development. Because of the success of Japanese economic development, Japanese management has become a unique and important field by itself. While Japanese management was much emulated in the 1980s and 1990s, it has also adapted and evolved according to chronological phases in its development. Changing Japanese economic needs, demographic shifts and evolving trends/lifestyles/habits seem to push Japanese management into a creative knowledge-based phase which is covered in the last section of this course. In the final analysis and in terms of application, one may develop comparative perspectives after taking this course, both internally within the different chronological phases of Japanese management as well as externally with other forms of management techniques.

This course introduces the historical and social development of present Japanese management practices and systems. It aims to equip students with better understanding of Japanese business people, to establish mutual understanding, and to help students learn to collaborate with staff from Japan so as to achieve common objectives.

The organization of the course is chronological starting from Japanese management’s basic features to revisiting its sustained and continued applicability and finally to cutting edge contemporary ideas. It is a broad survey intended for class participants with no prior knowledge of management theories or Japanese studies beyond the usual terms encountered in the mass media and some acquisitions of new terminologies. It does not pretend to be comprehensive but gives participants a broad and foundational understanding of J-management augmented by selected case studies. It is broad and foundational in the sense that the course can be divided into three phases from the basic features to recent case studies and then to the nuts and bolts of contemporary ideas.

Chinese course description:

本科目透過日本社會發展的觀點介紹現代日本管理學的歴史。旨在提昇學生對於日本管理人員的認識，謀求與日本人建立互相理解，互相信任和互相合作的精神，進而達成共同的工作目標。

Course schedule

=============================================================

Week 1: Basic concepts of Japanese management

Lecture:

Mandatory Reading:

(1) Haghirian, Parissa, "Chapter 2 Human Resource Management" in Understanding Japanese Management Practices (NY: Business Expert Press), 2010, pp. 13-30 (17 pages)

Tutorial:

The first tutorial will go through the requirements of the course. ================================================================

Tutorial:

Class participants will work in groups to discuss their understanding of the readings and learn to adopt the perspective of their training, expertise and interest to analyze the readings, ask questions and discuss it in the context of their research interests.

=======================================================================

Week 3:

Discussion on research essay also includes items like thesis statement/hypothesis; important issues and research questions for inclusion in their essays and a table of contents. courses.title: History of Japanese Literature

courses.description: The aim of this course is to provide students with a historical outline of Japanese literature. Major works and writers of different periods will be introduced through Chinese/English translation or the original works if students possess sufficient Japanese reading ability.
courses.learning_outcomes: Students are expected to understand the history of Japanese literature, and its influence to traditional and modern Japanese culture. They can appreciate classics written by Japanese writers, and analyse Japanese cultural phenomena with a critical perspective.

13 (5) Conclusion

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: The History of 20th Century Japan
courses.description: This course explores modern Japan, which underwent perhaps the most striking transformation of any country in the modern world. Narrowly escaping colonization, Japan transformed into one of the most important imperial and economic powers in the world. Modern Japan is also doubly fascinating for a comparative take on the great themes of modern global history: the social upheavals and transformations of capitalism and democracy, the rise and fall of modern imperialism, the experience of total war, and the spread of a mass consumer culture. This course examines the invention of new traditions and the construction of a new national identity, the rise and fall of empire, the devastation of World War II, and the astonishing postwar economic growth and puzzling stagnation in contemporary times. This course explores how people in Japan have dealt with the dilemmas of modernity that challenge us all.
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of the semester, students will have achieved:

(1) Understand the major dynamics and trends of modern Japanese history

(2) Deepen their understanding of modern and contemporary Japanese society

(3) Learn to write logical and coherent historical papers
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1: Course Introduction; Meiji Restoration

This will explore the arrival of the “Black Ships” and the reasons behind the overthrow of the Shogunate.

Week 2: Civilization and Enlightenment

This week deals with Japan’s Meiji-era cultural/intellectual Westernization.

Week 3: Constructing the Meiji State

This week explores the creation of Japan’s new political structure and the fruits of Japan’s modern revolution.

Week 4: “Rich Nation, Strong Army,” and the Making of Japan’s Empire

This week deals with the logic and goals of becoming an empire in the age of imperialism.

Week 5: State and Society in Japan’s Imperial Democracy

This week investigates the move toward party leadership, and the linkages between state and society in Taishō era Japan.

Week 6: The Roaring ‘20s, and Tensions in Modern Life

This week explores the tensions brought about by capitalism and Japanese modernity.

Week 7: Crisis of Capitalism and Empire

This week explores the Great Depression and the “Manchurian Incident.”

Week 8: Japan at War

This week deals with the “China Incident,” Japanese ultra-nationalism, and the move toward all-out war in the Asia-Pacific. It also deals with daily life during wartime.

Week 9: America’s Japan: Occupation and Aftermath

This week studies the American Occupation of Japan and its aftermath.

Week 10: Cold War Realignments and the Conservative Political Resurgence

This week highlights the remarkable conservative resurgence in Japan, and its battles with the progressive left.

Week 11: Postwar Economy and Society

This week deals with the growth of a middle class and meritocratic society in the postwar years, and the remarkable “economic miracle.”

Week 12: A New Global Power?

This week discusses the Plaza Accord, the Japanese economic bubble, and the birth of Japan as a new global power in the 1980s.

Week 13: The Lost Decades, 3.11, and Beyond

This week has students cover important issues from the last 25 years of Japanese society.
courses.feedback_evaluation: Student feedback will be collected through the course evaluation, a mid-term survey, and through informal discussion with the teacher.

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: The Construction of Japanese Ethnic and Cultural Identity
courses.description:
Though extensively influenced by ancient China, Japan has developed into one of the world’s most distinctive cultures. Japan’s remote geographic location made it possible for changes to come relatively slowly, and except for the postwar reform, most innovations resulted from the voluntary adoption of foreign cultures rather than external pressure.

This course discusses how Japanese civilization, i.e. the Yamato nation, and the Japanese language were formed. How Japanese society arose through the localization of foreign cultures will be one of the major themes covered. Efforts will be made to identify nation-wide characteristics, if any, and investigate how they were cultivated over the centuries. Special attention will be paid to the similarities that can be found between the two major social reforms in modern Japanese history, i.e. the Meiji Restoration and postwar reform, and to the question of how these reforms influence Japan society today.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Features of the Japanese Language: A Cultural and Historical Analysis

To discuss how Japanese people i.e. Jomon people, Yayoi people and Yamato nation and the Japanese language were possibly formed.

2. The Early Horse-rider Theory and the Modified Hypothesis

To discuss the pros and cons of the popular and yet controversial theory for analyzing how Yamato nation was formed.

3. Features of Japanese Cultural History

To introduce the features of Japanese cultural history i.e. the separation of power and authorities, top-down social reforms, cultural advancement by external stimulations, etc. How the Edo government ruled the people (only samurai and farmers will be focused) will also be discussed.

4. Essence of Japanese Aesthetics

To introduce makoto, aware, okashi, wabi & sabi, iki.

5. Zen and Japanese Tea Ceremony

To introduce how Japanese tea ceremony was developed in a Japanese way.

6. Rice Growing in Edo Period and the Correlations between the Government and the Farmers

To introduce how Japanese government helped and exploited the farmers in Edo period.

7. Double Structure of Japanese People’s Mentality in Meiji Period

To introduce how the country advanced but the people were confused in Meiji period.

8. The Ideal and Reality of the Peace Constitution Drawn up after the Second World War

To introduce how the Postwar Japan was reformed and changed under the overwhelming influence by the GHQ.

9. The Thematic Concerns of Imamura’s Ballad of Narayama and Its Implication to the Understanding of Japan Society

To analyze Imamura’s thematic concerns in the Ballad of Narayama, and discuss its implication to the understanding of Japan society.

1st week

Introduction of the course

2nd week

Features of the Japanese Language: A Cultural and Historical Analysis

3rd and 4th weeks

The Early Horse-rider Theory and the Modified Hypothesis

5th and 6th weeks

Features of Japanese Cultural History

7th and 8th weeks

Essence of Japanese Aesthetics

9th week

Zen and Japanese Tea Ceremony

10th week

Rice Growing in Edo Period and the Correlations between the Government and the Farmers

11th and 12th weeks

Double Structure of Japanese People’s Mentality in Meiji Period

13th week

The Ideal and Reality of the Peace Constitution Drawn up after the Second World War

14th week

The Thematic Concerns of Imamura’s Ballad of Narayama and Its Implication to the Understanding of Japan Society

15th week

Concluding remarks about the course

2. A course evaluation will be carried out by the end of each teaching term.

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Everyday Life and Cultural Representation in Modern Japan

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 405
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 405

courses.description: This course aims at examining the dynamics between everyday life, modernity, and the representation of tradition in modern Japan from two different perspectives. Part One of the course introduces students Japanese responses to the idea of modernity, and the cultural transformation and social crisis over the course of Japan’s modernization. Part Two examines the continuation of, rapprochement with, and appropriation of traditional elements in the cultural lives of contemporary Japan.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1) Students are expected to be able to critically discuss the ideas and effects of modernization in Japan in different aspects, such as gender, urban space, and environmental problems.

2) Students are expected to be able to critically analyze the representation of tradition in contemporary Japan.

3) Students are expected to develop their own critical angles to examine and interpret the meanings of Japanese cultural products, such as films, novels, and anime.

4) Students are expected to strengthen their reading skills in a range of academic disciplines, including anthropology, cultural studies, and modern history.
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1 Introduction

Week 2 Mass Culture and Modern Japan

Week 3 Gender and Modern Japan

Week 4 Tokyo: The Modern City

Week 5 War and Destruction

Week 6 Environmental Disasters

Week 7 Life and Death

Week 8 Looking Back: Reexamining Traditions in Contemporary Japan

Week 9 Screening the Past in the Present: Ozu’s Tokyo Story

Week 10 Otaku and Tradition

Week 11 Nostalgia and Consumption

Week 12 Nostalgia and Revitalization

Week 13 Conclusion: Postmodern Japan
courses.feedback_evaluation: Students are welcome to speak to the instructor about their views of the course. In addition, students will be asked to fill out a mid-term survey after about four weeks into the semester and a University course evaluation at the end of the semester.
courses.required_readings:

(2) Tipton, Elise K. 2012. “The Department Store: Producing Modernity in Interwar Japan.” In *Rethinking Japanese Modernism*, edited by Roy Starrs, 428-51. Leiden; Boston: Global Oriental.

(2) Tamanoi, Mariko Asano. 1998. “The City and the Countryside: Competing Taishō ‘Modernities’ on Gender.” In *Japan’s Competing Modernities: Issues in Culture and Democracy 1900-1930*, edited by Sharon A. Minichiello, 91-113. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press.

Week 4 Tokyo: The Modern City

(1) Fujii, James A. 2004. “Introduction: Refiguring the Modern: Maeda Ai and the City.” In *Text and the City: Essays on Japanese Modernity*, by Ai Maeda, James A. Fujii, Rey Chow, Harry Harootunian, and Masao Miyoshi, 1-17. Durham: Duke University Press

(2) Maeda, Ai. 2004. “Asakusa as Theatre: Kawabata Yasunari’s *The Crimson Gang of Asakusa.*” Translated by Edward Fowler. In *Text and the City: Essays on Japanese Modernity*, by Ai Maeda, James A. Fujii, Rey Chow, Harry Harootunian, and Masao Miyoshi, 145-61. Durham: Duke University Press.

(2) Peterson, Nathan J. 2013. “Adapting Religious Practice in Response to Disaster in Iwate Prefecture. In *Japan Copes with Calamity: Ethnographies of the Earthquake, Tsunami and Nuclear Disasters of March 2011*, edited by Tom Gill, Brigitte Steger, and David H. Slater, 77-98. New York, NY: Peter Lang.

(2) Suzuki, Hikaru. 2000. “The History of Japanese Funeral Traditions.” In *The Price of Death: The Funeral Industry in Contemporary Japan*, 39-59. Stanford, Calif.: Stanford University Press.

(2) Kikuchi, Yuko. 2004. "Reverse Orientalism: The Development of *Mingei* Theory into National and International Modernism." In *Japanese Modernisation and Mingei Theory: Cultural Nationalism and Oriental Orientalism*, 197-241. London: RoutledgeCurzon.

(2) Philips, Alastair. 2003. "Pictures of the Past in the Present: Modernity, Femininity and Stardom in the Postwar Films of Ozu Yasujiro." *Screen* 44(2): 154-66.

Week 10 Otaku and Tradition

(1) Murakami, Takashi. 2000 “The Super Flat Manifesto” and “A Theory of Super Flat Japanese Art.” In *Super Flat*, 5, 9-25. Tokyo: MADRA Publishing.

(2) Steinberg, Marc. 2004. “*Otaku* Consumption, Superflat Art and the Return to Edo.” *Japan Forum* 16(3): 449-71.

Week 11 Nostalgia and Consumption

(1) Creighton, Millie. 1997. “Consuming Rural Japan: The Marketing of Tradition and Nostalgia in the Japanese Travel Industry.” *Ethnology* 36(3): 239-54.

(2) Ivy, Marilyn. 1995. “Itineraries of Knowledge: Trans-Figuring Japan.” In *Discourses of the Vanishing: Modernity, Phantasm, Japan*, 29-65. Chicago: University of Chicago Press.

Week 12 Nostalgia and Revitalization

(1) Breslin, Lynne. 2015. “The Art of Tourism in Rural Japan: Footnotes of an Itinerant Architect.” In *Art Place Japan: The Echigo-Tsumari Art Triennale and the Vision to Reconnect Art and Nature*, by Fram Kitagawa, 259-81. New York: Princeton Architectural Press.

(2) Kitagawa, Fram. 2015. “The Echigo-Tsumari Art Triennale: How It All Began” and “The Echigo-Tsumari Region.” In *Art Place Japan: The Echigo-Tsumari Art Triennale and the Vision to Reconnect Art and Nature*, 9-27. New York: Princeton Architectural Press.

Week 13 Conclusion: From Postmodern Japan to the End of Heisei

(1) Asada, Akira. 1989. “Infantile Capitalism and Japan’s Postmodernism: A Fairy Tale.” In *Postmodernism and Japan*, edited by Masao Miyoshi and Harry D. Harootunian, 273-8. Durham: Duke University Press.

(2) Leheby, David. 2022. “The Loss of Nostalgia, Not the Nostalgia of Loss: Or, What Happens in Heisei Stays in Heisei.” In *Japan in the Heisei Era (1989-2019): Multidisciplinary Perspectives*, edited by Noriko Murai, Jeff Kingston, and Tina Burrett, 316-27. Abingdon, Oxon: Routledge.
courses.recommended_readings:
Week 1 Introduction

(1) Mishima, Yukio. [1970] 1971. “An Appeal.” Translated by Harris I. Martin. *Japan Interpreter* 7(1): 73-7.

(2) Natsume, Soseki. [1911] 2011. “The Civilization of Modern-Day Japan.” Translated by Jay Rubin. In *The Columbia Anthology of Modern Japanese Literature*, edited by J. Thomas Rimer and Van C. Gessel, 154-60. New York: Columbia University Press.

Week 2 Mass Culture and Modern Japan

(1) Marshall, Amy Bliss. 2019. “‘We Came, We Saw, We Astonished’: How a Japanese Mass Was Won.” In *Magazines and the Making of Mass Culture in Japan,*77-128. Toronto: University of Toronto Press.

(2) Moeran, Brian. 1998. "The Birth of the Japanese Department Store." In *Asian Department Stores*, edited by Kerrie L. MacPherson, 141-76. London: Routledge.

Week 3 Gender and Modern Japan

(1) Karlin, Jason G. 2014. “Introduction: Nationalism, Everyday Life, and the Myth of Eternal Return.” In *Gender and Nation in Meiji Japan: Modernity, Loss, and the Doing of History*, 7-18. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press.

(2) Yano. Christine R. 2013. “‘Flying Geisha’: Japanese Stewardesses with Pan American World Airways.” In *Modern Girls on the Go: Gender, Mobility, and Labor in Japan*, edited by Alissa Freedman, Laura Miller, and Christine R. Yano, 85-106. Palo Alto: Stanford University Press.

Week 4 Tokyo: The Modern City

(1) Smith, Henry D. 1978. “Tokyo as an Idea: An Exploration of Japanese Urban Thought until 1945.” *The Journal of Japanese Studies* 4(1): 45-80.

(2) Waley, Paul. 2002. “Moving the Margins of Tokyo.” *Urban Studies* 39(9): 1533-50.

Week 5 War and Destruction

(1) Sanders, Holly. 2012 “Panpan: Streetwalking in Occupied Japan.” *Pacific Historical Review* 81(3): 404-31.

(2) Takenaka, Akiko. 2016. “Gender and Post-war Relief: Support for War-Widowed Mothers in Occupied Japan (1945–52).” *Gender & History* 28(3): 775-93.

Week 6 Environmental Disasters

(1) McCormack, Gavan, and Fusako Yoshinaga. 2004. "Minamata: The Irresponsibility of the Japanese State." *The Asia-Pacific Journal* 2(12).

(2) Notehelfer, F. G. 1975. "Japan's First Pollution Incident." *The Journal of Japanese Studies* 1(2): 351-83.

Week 7 Life and Death

(1) Lock, Margaret. 1998. “Deadly Disputes: Hybrid Selves and the Calculation of Death in Japan and North America.” *Osiris* 13: 410-29.

(2) Miyazaki, Michiko. 2007. “The History of Abortion-Related Acts and Current Issues in Japan.” *Medicine and Law* 26(4): 791-800.

Week 8 Looking Back: Reexamining Traditions in Contemporary Japan

(1) Rausch, Anthony. 2014. “Regionalism and Local.” In *The SAGE Handbook of Modern Japanese Studies*, edited by James D. Babb, 148-65. Los Angeles: SAGE 2014.

(2) Robertson, Jennifer. 1988. "Furusato Japan: The Culture and Politics of Nostalgia." *International Journal of Politics, Culture, and Society* 1(4): 494-518.

Week 9 Screening the Past in the Present: Ozu’s *Tokyo Story*

(1) Cairns, Graham. 2014. “Historical Cinematic Space: The Architecture of Culture in Jean Renoir's *Le Grande Illusion* and Yasujiro Ozu's *Tokyo Story*.” *Film & History: An Interdisciplinary Journal* 44(2): 22-44.

Week 10 Otaku and Tradition

(1) Azuma, Hiroki. 2009. “The Otaku’s Pseudo-Japan” In *Otaku: Japan's Database Animals*, 3-24. Minneapolis: University of Minnesota Press.

(2) Gluck, Carol. 1998. “The Invention of Edo.” In *Mirror of Modernity: Invented Tradition of Modern Japan*, edited by Stephen Vlastos, 262-84. Berkeley, Calif.: University of California Press.

Week 11 Nostalgia and Consumption

(1) Creighton, Millie. 1998. "Pre-industrial Dreaming in Post-industrial Japan: Department Stores and the Commoditization of Community Traditions." *Japan Forum* 10(2): 127-49.

(2) Cwiertka, Katarzyna J. 2018. "Serving the Nation: The Myth of *Washoku*." In *Consuming Life in Post-Bubble Japan: A Transdisciplinary Perspective*, edited by Kataryna J. Cwiertka and Ewa Machotka, 89-106. Amsterdam: Amsterdam University Press.

Week 12 Nostalgia and Revitalization

(1) Evans, Neil. 2002. “Machi-zukuri as a New Paradigm in Japanese Urban Planning: Reality or Myth?” *Japan Forum* 14(3): 443-64.

(2) Klien, Susanne. 2010. “Contemporary Art and Regional Revitalisation: Selected Artworks in the Echigo-Tsumari Art Triennial 2000-6.” *Japan Forum* 22(3-4): 513-543.

Week 13 Conclusion: From Postmodern Japan to the End of Heisei

(1) Harootunian, H.D.1989. “Visible Discourses/Invisible Ideologies.” In *Postmodernism and Japan*, edited by Masao Miyoshi and Harry D. Harootunian, 63-92. Durham: Duke University Press.

(2) Hirano, Kyoko. 2022. “Cinema's Uneasy Social Critique: The Heisei Era Onscreen.” In *Japan in the Heisei Era (1989-2019): Multidisciplinary Perspectives*, edited by Noriko Murai, Jeff Kingston, and Tina Burrett, 272-84. Abingdon, Oxon: Routledge.

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Practical Business Japanese
courses.description: This course is designed to help students improve their Japanese business conversation and reading comprehension through practice in business Japanese and reading business-related essays. The course also emphasizes Japanese business culture to help students behave properly in interacting with Japanese business people. Students are required to pass a placement test or obtain equivalent competence in Japanese before course registration. Advisory: Not for students who have studied for one year or more in Japan.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Prerequisites: JASP1040,2460 or 2480 or equivalent competence in Japanese
Not for students who have taken JASP4180,4310,4330,4350,4360,4370,4380,4390,4440,4450,4510,4520
courses.learning_outcomes:
This course is designed to help students improve their Japanese business conversation and reading comprehension through practice in business Japanese and reading business related essays. The course also emphasizes Japanese business culture to help students behave properly in interacting with Japanese business people.

After Completing this course students are expected to be able to:

1. introduce themselves in a business occasion in Japanese

2. speak and behave properly in the following business occasions

a. at the first meeting with a Japanese client

b. when making a phone call

c. when receiving a phone call

d. when receiving a message from a client

e. when passing a message to a colleague

f. when requesting

g. when declining a request

h. when taking a client out for dinner or drinks

3. understand Japanese business culture

4. speak impressively in a job talk

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Selected Topics in Japanese Grammar
courses.description: This course will focus on the grammatical structure and characteristics of the Japanese language. Students will renew their grammatical understanding of Japanese and analyze it from a systematic point of view. Some of the major parts of speech, such as nouns, adverbs, adjectives, Rentaishi, particles, including their significance, characteristics, and functions will also be covered and discussed.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Prerequisites: JASP2460 or 2480 or equivalent competence in Japanese
Not for students who have taken JASP4180,4310,4330,4350,4360,4370,4380,4390,4440,4450,4510,4520
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Students are expected to be able to describe the grammatical structure and the characteristics of the Japanese language.
2. Students are expected to be able to make use of the methods learnt to analyze Japanese grammar.
3. Students are expected to be able to use nouns, adverbs, adjective, *Rentaishi*, and particles, in appropriate and accurate ways.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. What are the characteristics of the Japanese language?

2. Interesting nouns in Japanese (e.g. nouns without any meanings, extremely long noun phases, etc)

3. The transformation of おはようございます (“Good morning” in Japanese)

4. Making good use of them to make your Japanese more authentic: Adverbs

5. The part of speech with unique origin and usages: Keiyoudoushi (na-adjective)

6. The unique part of speech in Japanese: *Rentaishi*

7. The part of speech that is very important but often get omitted: particles
courses.feedback_evaluation:
Course and Teaching Evaluation Survey

One month survey

3. 今井新悟（2011）『日本語多義語学習辞典　形容詞・副詞編』アルク
4. 田近洵一（2012）『くわしい国文法』文英堂
5. 中西久実子・庵功雄『日本語文法演習　助詞 ―「は」と「が」、複合格助詞、とりたて助詞など―』スリーエーネットワーク

Lecture 13

Presentation of each group and written examination.

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Japanese Improvement Strategies- Vocabularies, Grammar, Listening and Reading

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 405

courses.description: This course is mainly designed for students who want to achieve a high standard of Japanese language proficiency in vocabulary, grammar, listening and reading. Specifically, it will focus on strategies aimed at language improvement for achieving a publicly recognized standard (which is equivalent to JPLT N2 level ), as well as encouraging students to be responsible for managing their own language learning. Coursework will include vocabulary, grammar, listening and reading assignments. Students are required to pass a placement test or obtain equivalent competence in Japanese before course registration. Advisory: Not for students who have studied for one year or more in Japan. Not for students who possess JLPT (N1 or N2) qualification.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Students should have passed JLPT (N3) (certificate issued within 3 years upon the day of registration), or passed JASP3340 placement test
Not for students who have taken JASP4180,4310,4330,4350,4360,4370,4380,4390,4440,4450,4510,4520
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing JASP3340, students are expected to be able to:

1) understand the meaning and know the pronunciation of different vocabularies and Kanji of JLPT N2 level.

2) understand the grammar patterns covered by the JLPT N2 level.

3) read and understand upper-intermediate level Japanese articles (from daily articles to official documents) by using different kinds of reading skills.

4) understand a wide range of spoken Japanese (from daily conversations to formal speech) by using different kinds of listening skills.

1 通過此課程，進一步學習日語，掌握各類常用詞語和句型的應用；

2 通過短句和短文的翻譯練習，初步掌握翻譯技巧；

3 通過課堂討論，加深對日語的認識和對日本文化的理解；

4 通過視聽翻譯練習，認識日本人的日常用語習慣。
courses.course_syllabus:
第一週
導言
①　課程目的
②　翻譯標準
③　作業評閲基準

自第二週起
翻譯技巧概述
①　詞序的轉換
②　詞性的轉換
③　詞議的引伸
④　減詞
⑤　増詞
⑥　詞議的搭配
⑦　詞議的色彩
⑧　詞議的正反表達
⑨　詞的轉義
⑩　等値翻譯

以上毎個章節均附有相應的練習在課堂上做。各重點學習項目之間，會加插常用句型的練習和派課外作業。

自第十週起
①　分組在課堂上發表譯作-

小組人數：２～３人，自由組合。譯作發表前和導師一起進行小組討論。
②　習作講解-

由導師個別講解個人習作。
③　視聽練習-

觀看影片（動畫片、電視劇、電影），練習視聽翻譯。
④　測驗
courses.feedback_evaluation:
① 開課後一個月進行意見調查

② 期末課程評估

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Chinese-Japanese Translation
courses.description: Through the discussion of common problems in translating Chinese into Japanese, students will learn more about common methods, skills and expressions in Chinese-Japanese translation, thus enabling them to have a clearer understanding and basic knowledge of Chinese-Japanese translation. Students are required to pass a placement test or obtain equivalent competence in Japanese before course registration. Advisory: Not for students who have studied for one year or more in Japan.

courses.feedback_evaluation: Students are always welcomed to provide comments and/or feedback on the course via email or any other appropriate channel such as Blackboard. Also, students can convey these messages personally to the teacher by using the comment space of each assignment. It is possible and welcomed to make an appointment with the teacher and personally discuss their own problem(s) about the course or studying Japanese in general. The teacher may distribute a brief questionnaire as needed to obtain comments and feedback from the students.

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Japanese Through Media
courses.description: This course is designed for students who want to improve their language proficiency and vocabulary through various media sources available in the Hong Kong market. Focus will be put on encouraging students to be responsible for managing their Japanese language learning through resources available in their daily life. Students are required to pass a placement test or obtain equivalent competence in Japanese before course registration. Advisory: Not for students who have studied for one year or more in Japan.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Prerequisites: JASP1040, 2460 or 2480 or equivalent competence in Japanese
Not for students who have taken JASP4180,4310,4330,4350,4360,4370,4380,4390,4440,4450,4510,4520
courses.learning_outcomes:
- Deepen basic skills to access and understand media information in Japanese.

- Familiarise the style and structure of Japanese that commonly used in media.

- Widen student’s understanding of the cultural context of those vocabularies used in media products, such as in Mixi,

facebook, twitter, YouTube, etc.

- Enable students to have the ability to express their opinion on the information in mass media.

- Develop student’s ability on understanding “Emergency" information such as for typhoon, earthquake and traffic information,

and so be ready to travel or study in Japan.
courses.course_syllabus:
The semester will be planned according to the following areas:

- Emergency Information: learning and understanding the instructions and vocabularies used in different occasions of

emergency.

- Written Style and Spoken Style: compare the basic structure and grammatical patterns used in formal news articles and in

casual conversations in newspaper, TV drama and films.

- Communication in Internet: enhance the ability to communicate with native Japanese through internet using various internet

tools. Assess and review the grammar used in internet.

- Listening: using audio-visual materials so that students can learn about the cultural background of those daily

conversational topics and current issues.

- Workshop: students will be asked to do a small project to be presented in class, so to deepen the knowledge learned and

enhance their communication ability.

The lecturer will make frequent reference to the information in various media like news, TV drama, film, internet etc.
courses.feedback_evaluation:
- Survey after the first month of class.

- Course evaluation, which will take place around last week of the semester.
courses.required_readings: - No required readings, handout will be given in class.
courses.recommended_readings: - Printed materials, such as newspapers and magazines written in Japanese. (Those are available in CU libraries)

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Japan and Hong Kong

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 405

courses.description: This course investigates the relationships between Japan and Hong Kong from historical and sociological perspectives, focusing on the activities of the Japanese in Hong Kong from the mid-nineteenth century to the present. Main topics for lecture and discussion include early Japanese residents in prewar Hong Kong, Hong Kong under Japan's occupation, Japan-Hong Kong economic and cultural ties, the Japanese community in Hong Kong, the Japanese popular culture boom in Hong Kong and the Hong Kong culture in Japan. Guest lecture, panel discussion, study tour and debate may be organized to supplement lectures and discussion.
courses.learning_outcomes:
(1) 學習日本與香港歷史，了解港日交流的各種發展與現況

(2) 探究日本文化對香港的衝擊及本地化過程，從而對當前的生活背景有更深體會

(3) 了解香港流行文化在日本的流行情況，展望亞洲創意工業機緣與前景
courses.course_syllabus:
第一週 導論：課程簡介
討論：「我的日本觀」
記錄片：「星光伴我行之日本行」第3集上（10分）

「尋找他鄉的故事：日本篇」第24集（23分）

第二週 中日政治外交關係與香港
參考：《擺盪在兩岸之間》, pp. 147-157, 198-211

《知日部屋》之政治及社會篇, pp. 220-253

第三週 辯論
辯論：「中日關係前景是否樂觀？」

第四週 中日經貿關係與香港
記錄片：「戰後六十年」（22分）

參考：＜20世紀90年代中日經貿關係的發展與特點＞ , pp. 28-45

＜中日貿易結構與產業競爭＞ , pp. 73-83

第二部份：歷史篇

第五週 戰前港日關係
參考： “Making of a Japanese Community in Prewar Period 1841-1941,” pp. 111-132

《日本與亞洲華人社會》, pp. 133-148

第六週 日治時期的香港
記錄片：「抗日戰爭與日治香港」 （19分）

參考：《日佔時期的香港》, pp. 103-108, 123-153

第三部份：當代社經篇

第七週 現代港日政治與經濟的連繫
記錄片：「多多指教：香港日資百貨公司」（23分）

參考：《中日關係與亞太區域合作》, pp. 111-123

第八週 日本對香港的文化外交與香港的日本人社會
記錄片：「日人白書」（23分）

口頭報告：(1)「我的留日/旅日經驗」或 (2) 一個居港日人的短訪

參考：“The Japanese Association and the Kowloon Club,” pp. 75-92

第九週 中期考試與參觀
參觀：香港歷史博物館（星期六上午11時-12時半）

第四部份：當代文化篇

第十週 香港的日本流行文化（上）: 動漫

參考：＜日本漫畫對香港漫畫界及流行文化的影響＞, pp. 105-112

第十一週 香港的日本流行文化（中）: 電影

參考：＜日本色情電影在香港的歷史考察＞, pp. 149-166

第十二週 香港的日本流行文化（下）: 音樂與電視劇

參考：＜香港的日本電視劇：歷史與影響＞, pp. 182-199

第十三週 日本的香港流行文化
參考：＜日本的金庸現象初探＞, pp. 131-146

參考：《知日部屋》之跨文化交流篇, pp. 173-203
courses.feedback_evaluation:
One-month survey

Course evaluation

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Japanese Youth Cultures in Asia
courses.description: Youth cultures have played a critical role in shaping Japanese public attitudes and debates, consumer movements, and institutions in the postwar period. Japanese youth-oriented popular cultures are currently reshaping the cultural landscape across much of Asia. This course explores Japan's youth cultures and introduces the diversity of youth experiences in Japan. It further allows students to explore the influence of Japanese youth cultures in other Asian societies. The course encourages students to compare youth cultures in Japan with that of China, Hong Kong, and North America. The course will also consider the rise of transnational youth cultures based on movements of people, ideas, and images across national borders. It will explore the emergence of a Japan influenced youth popular culture in Asia as well as the particular local contexts in which the experiences of youth take on meanings for individuals.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Analyze the nature and influence of the knowledge from Japanese Studies
2. Evaluate how Japanese culture influence the generation’s ideology
3. Understand how concepts of Japanese-ness are established and developed
4. Elaborate how Japanese culture is localized and metamorphosed after participations of different stakeholders and local agencies.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. 日本作為概念： 研究的重要性和可行性
2. 研究方法簡析： 深度訪談、田野考察、文本研讀
3. 田野考察的作法
4. 型男／美女／身體政治
5. 原宿系？／ＭＫ 系？
6. 電影分享會／中期習作：觀看短片，回答問題一條。答案字數不得超過一千字。
7. 偶像文化淺析 (1)
8. 男同性戀漫畫文本於亞洲之影響
9. 手提電話與網路文化衝擊：教育泛式和意識形態建立
10. 總結：日本青少年文化在亞洲代表什麼價值？
courses.feedback_evaluation: Students are welcome to speak to the teacher about their views of the course. In addition, students will be asked to fill out a mid-term survey after about four weeks into the semester and a University course evaluation at the end of the semester.

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Intermediate Japanese through Listening and Reading
courses.description: This course introduces intermediate Japanese vocabularies, expressions and grammar patterns through lively listening and reading activities on different topics. Introduction of Japanese daily life and culture will also be included. Teaching materials include short comics, videos, listening exercises, animation clips and articles. Students are required to pass a placement test or obtain equivalent competence in Japanese before course registration. Advisory: Not for students who have studied for one year or more in Japan.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Pre-requisite: JASP1040 or 2460 or 2480 or equivalent competence in Japanese.
Not for students who have taken JASP4180,4310,4330,4350,4360,4370,4380,4390,4440,4450,4510,4520
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. After finishing the course, students should be able to use the appropriate listening and reading skills and strategies to improve comprehension.

2. Students will have broadened their knowledge of Japanese vocabularies, expressions, and grammar patterns through assigned reading and listening teaching materials (e.g. magazine articles, short comics, listening exercises, and video clips).

3. Students will have improved their Japanese listening and reading proficiency through the above exercises.

4. Students will have acquired a deeper knowledge of the usage of Japanese language in context with an enriched understanding of up-to-date usages and social situations.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Course introduction

2. Introduction of different skills and strategies of listening and reading

3. Listening and reading exercises related to the following topics:

- Japanese language usage and restrictions by contexts

(Uses of Japanese according to a person’s status or position, Taboo in Japanese… etc.)

- Vocabulary which enriches the vividness of expression in Japanese

(Onomatopoeia, mimetic words… etc.)

- Up to date Japanese vocabularies and expressions

(Trendy vocabulary, commonly used vocabulary in youth magazines, vocabulary and expressions commonly used by youngsters… etc.)

- Japanese vocabularies related to Japanese modern daily life and traditional culture

4. Examination and student presentations
courses.feedback_evaluation:
One month survey

Course evaluation

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Japanese Language and Society
courses.description: This course explores both linguistic structures and sociolinguistic features of the Japanese language. The first half of the course will discuss sociolinguistic features in Japanese language such as the history of linguistic exchanges in the kanji (Chinese character) and lexicon between China and Japan among other case studies, application of linguistic politeness expressions and word formation. The second half of the course will discuss the major linguistic features in Japanese language such as its grammatical structures and semantic features in the Japanese language. Linguistic approaches will be the principal analytical tools employed in this course. This course is designed for students who have completed elementary Japanese language courses and are interested to know more about the structure of the Japanese language and its sociolinguistic characteristics. At the completion of the course, students will have gained a better understanding of the structure of the Japanese language in general situations and will have learned how to apply language usage according to Japanese linguistic social norms.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Students taking this course should possess Japanese Language Proficiency Test (JLPT) N2 certificate or having Japanese language proficiency equivalent to JLPT N2 level; or should have completed JASP2480/3340.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1 Students should acquire knowledge of the Japanese language structure

(i.e. sentence structure, characteristics of words and sound system)

2 Students should be able to describe the major characteristics of kanji, kanji words and loanwords in the Japanese

language

3 Students should be able to explain the characteristics and usage of the linguistic politeness in the Japanese

language

4 Students should be able to explain the main characteristics of the lexicon and word formation in Japanese

language

5 Students should acquire knowledge of syntactic and semantic structures in Japanese language
courses.course_syllabus:
Topic 1 Introduction to the structure of Japanese language

In this section, the structure of the Japanese language will be discussed. It analyzes sentence structure, characteristics of words and sound systems in the Japanese language.

Topic 2 The development of kanji, kanji words and loanwords in the Japanese language

In this section, the historical overview of kanji in Japanese language will be discussed. Various types of reading (goon, kanon and tôon – different forms of reading in Japanese) in Japanese kanji will be introduced. In addition, the history of how kanji words and loanwords are developed in the Japanese language will be discussed.

Topic 3 Expressions of linguistic politeness in the Japanese language

In this section, the category and various forms of phrases expressing linguistic politeness will be discussed. It includes honorific expressions, expressions used in making requests, making apologies, expressing gratitude and personal opinions, giving advice and asking for permission. Emphasis will be placed on when and how these expressions are used. The relationship between such expressions and the characteristics of hierarchy in Japanese society will also be discussed.

Topic 4 Lexicon and words formation in the Japanese language

In this section, the word type and word formation in Japanese language will be discussed. Special attention will be given to:

a. compound words

b. onomatopoeia (words signifying sounds and gestures)

Topic 5 Syntax in the Japanese language

In this section, the syntax structure in Japanese language will be discussed. Some of the highlighted items include:

a. tense and aspectual system

b. verb classification

c. “Mondaina Nihongo” (problematic Japanese usage)

Topic 6 Semantics in Japanese language

In this section, the semantic structure in Japanese language will be discussed. Methodology such as semantic analysis, differentiation of connotative meaning and denotative meaning, and idiomatic expressions will be introduced.
courses.feedback_evaluation:
1 One month survey

2 Term-end Course and Teaching Evaluation

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Japanese Philosophy
courses.description: This course aims to understand and analyze Japanese philosophy, which is an important topic for reflecting on the roots of Japanese intellectual history and contemporary philosophical debates. Three areas will be discussed: 1) Japanese intellectual traditions such as those found in Buddhism, Confucianism and Shinto/Native Studies thought; 2) modern Japanese philosophers such as Nishida Kitaro, Kuki Shuzo and Watsuji Tetsuro; and 3) contemporary Japanese thought: Karatani Kojin, Post-311 philosophy, etc.
courses.learning_outcomes: Upon completion of the course, students are expected to understand basic concepts of Japanese philosophy, and their influence on traditional, modern and contemporary Japanese culture. They can critically read texts written by Japanese philosophers, and analyze Japanese cultural phenomena from a philosophical perspective.
courses.course_syllabus: 1. Introduction 2. Traditional Japanese thought: Buddhism, Confucianism, Shinto and Native Studies 3. Modern Japanese philosophy: Nishida Kitaro, Kuki Shuzo, Watsuji Tetsuro 4. Contemporary Japanese thought: Karatani Kojin, Post-311 philosophy 5. Conclusion
courses.feedback_evaluation:
Mid-term survey, and

Course evaluation.

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Advanced Business Japanese
courses.description: This course aims at further developing students’ knowledge in Japanese business environment and culture and proficiency in business Japanese. This course will help students improve their capabilities in reading Japanese business materials, writing commercial letters, and expressing their opinions through discussions on specific topics. Students are required to pass a placement test or obtain equivalent competence in Japanese before course registration.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of the subject , students will be able to:

a. Understand the business communication in Japanese company.

b. Grasp the honorific word and use business communication.

c. Identify the gaps among the culture of Japanese, Hong Kong and China business.

d. More practice of business communication in Japanese company will be applied in the class.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. About of Interview.

2. More practical letter and e-mail

3. About of Japanese Business

4. Business presentation

5. About of Japanese company’s internal relationship.

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Advanced English-Japanese Translation
courses.description: This course introduces some of the key concepts in translation theory and focuses on their application to English to Japanese translation practice. The course provides students who have a solid knowledge of basic Japanese with a practical experience of translation and aims to develop their translation skills to a high level of proficiency. Students will work with a wide range of text types, become familiar with different translation techniques, and develop intellectual perspectives on the activity of translation. Texts are drawn from various sources including news media, internet, business communication, academic literature, fiction and films. Students are required to pass a placement test or obtain equivalent competence in Japanese before course registration.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Prerequisite: One year of study in Japan or equivalent competence in Japanese
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Apply linguistic and sociocultural knowledge to produce translations from English to Japanese.
2. Recognize text types and their conventions, formulate best approaches for translating different types of texts, and reflect on strategies for problem solving.
3. Develop the ability to reflect on the process of translation.
4. Learn to analyze the relationship between translation and culture.
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1: Introduction to translation studies

Week 2: Skills and knowledge necessary to work as a successful translator

Week 3: Translation strategies, methods, approaches, and different degrees of freedom (literal, free, semantic, communicative, or adaptation)

Week 4: Techniques for translating different text types (literary, academic, business, and media)

Week 5: Techniques commonly adopted in English – Japanese translation (omission, expansion, and changing parts of text)

Week 6: Difficulties specific to English – Japanese translation (rhetorical devices, proverbs, and idioms)

Week 7: Methods for translating culture-specific items (transcription, naturalization, localization, and cultural equivalent)

Week 8: Translation and culture, interlingual translation and intralingual translation

Week 9: Computer-aided human translation and machine translation

Week 10: Oral dimension, consecutive interpreting and simultaneous interpreting

Week 11: Group presentation

Week 12: Group presentation

Week 13: Review and conclusions
courses.feedback_evaluation:
* Assignment: Students will be assigned one piece of homework (500 words) to practice their translation skills.
* Translation project: Each student will produce a translation of a set text of 1000 words accompanied by a 400-word reflective commentary outlining the student’s self-reflections on the strategies and procedures that s/he has employed for this translation project. The commentary may also address such questions as: How is your experience of doing this project related to what you have learned about translation during this course? In what way(s) has this project been beneficial for you? How might it be beneficial in the future, both for you and for others?
* Presentation: Students will work in groups of 3-4 members on a translation exercise relating to a topic of their choosing. Each group will translate an English text of 2000 words into Japanese and present to the whole class a summary of the work they have undertaken in the process of planning the project, researching the topic and translating the text.

In general, the summary should introduce the text (What is the text you chose to translate? Why did you choose this text? What do you particularly like about this text? What does your reader need to know about the author of the text? What does your reader need to know about the context from which the source text emerged?, etc.), and address the group’s choice of translation approach(es) and difficulties encountered in the exercise in both general terms and specific to English – Japanese translation (What are the conventions of the chosen text type? What similar texts did you use as your reference? What difficulties did you anticipate in the translation? What theoretical approach to translation did you follow? What translation strategies and techniques did you adopt and why? Did you encounter cultural problems in your translation? What process did you follow in preparing the translation? How did you solve the anticipated problems? Are you satisfied with your solutions? Where are you dissatisfied? Who or what sources helped you? How did you organize your work as a group?, etc.).

* Active participation: Assessed on each student’s engagement in class discussions, groupwork and practical translation tasks in-class.

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Advanced Spoken Japanese
courses.description: The course aims to enhance both speaking and listening abilities. Selected readings on contemporary Japanese or local social issues will be used to practice the expressions and exchange of ideas. Classroom activities will include exchanging ideas, discussion, speech, etc. Students are required to pass a placement test or obtain equivalent competence in Japanese before course registration.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Prerequisite: One year of study in Japan or equivalent competence in Japanese
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students should be able to:

・discuss some abstract contents in Japanese.

・discuss topics in Japanese not only in daily life level but also in business level for the future career.

・make discussion supported with evidence.
courses.course_syllabus:
In order to achieve this goal, this course is divided into three stages. In the first stage, students are required to present their points of views with reasoning after given a topic without referring to any materials. In this stage, students will be asked to be paired-up. They have to try to tell their points of views. and at the same time, they learn other’s opinions as well.

In the second stage, students will be asked to find subjective materials to support their objective ideas. They are asked to collect information that they need to support their thoughts and their partners are asked to do the same in order to argue their point of views with facts and references.

In the final stage, by using what have been learnt in the first and second stages, students will be asked to debate on abstract topics such as "Environmental Protection and Urban Development", "pros and cons of surrogate mother system" and " Problem of male and female students living together in the college dorm". Debate topics will be discussed and chosen by students so as to increase their interests and motivation on the course.
courses.feedback_evaluation:
・enforcement of questionnaires from students after course start one month.

・both exchange by an email

・free discussion for the theme choice.
courses.required_readings: There is not special requirement.
courses.recommended_readings:
Collection of materials related to three topics

・Various materials in Internet

・Materials such as newspapers, magazines and books in library

・Materials which teacher offers

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Advanced Japanese Improvement Strategies-Grammar & Listening Comprehension

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 706
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 706

courses.description: This course is designed to improve Japanese competence for students who want to achieve a higher standard of Japanese language proficiency in grammar and listening comprehension. It focuses on strategies aimed at language improvement for achieving a publicly recognized standard, as well as encouraging students to be responsible for managing their own language learning. Coursework includes listening and vocabulary assignments. Students are required to pass a placement test or obtain equivalent competence in Japanese before course registration.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Prerequisite: One year of study in Japan or passed Japanese Proficiency Test Level 2 or equivalent competence in Japanese.
courses.learning_outcomes: This course is designed to improve students’ grammatical knowledge and listening skills. At the same time, the course prepares students to manage their own language study after graduation. After taking the course, students have enough knowledge and preparation to pass JLPT (Japanese Language Proficient Test) N1.

courses.description: This course is the final year project for senior year entry students in Japanese Studies. It allows senior year entry students to assemble and analyze research materials collected as part of their final year project. This course is to be taken in the final year of attendance only (for alternative arrangements, special permission must be obtained from the department), and will be held alongside JASP 4602.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Pre-requisite: JASP 1090, JASP 2010, JASP 2030
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Students should be able to plan, develop, and implement a research project.
2. Students should be able to analyze scholarship written in Japanese, Chinese, and English, and make reasoned conclusions based off that scholarship.
3. Students should be able to collect original data, analyze, and communicate the results of the analysis.
courses.course_syllabus: This is an independent study, final-year project.
courses.feedback_evaluation: Students are encouraged to speak to their supervisors or the course coordinator about their views of the course. In addition, students will be asked to fill out a survey and a university course evaluation at the end of the semester.
courses.required_readings: N/A. This is an independent field-research project, so readings will vary for each student.
courses.recommended_readings: N/A

courses.description: This course enables students to assemble and analyse research materials collected during their year of study in Japan. Advisory: For Japanese Studies Majors who have studied in Japan. Course to be taken in their final year of attendance only.
courses.learning_outcomes:
At the completion of this course, students shall be capable of the following:

1. Planning, developing, and implementing a long-term (two-year) project.
2. Approaching people to make new contacts and creating networks.
3. Collecting experts’ research results written in Japanese, Chinese, and English, analyzing this material, making reasoned conclusions, and clearly communicating the results of the analysis orally and in writing.
4. Collecting original data, analyzing, and communicating the results of the analysis.
courses.course_syllabus: It is a final-year project.
courses.feedback_evaluation: Students are welcome to speak to their supervisors or the course coordinator about their views of the course. In addition, students will be asked to fill out a mid-term survey after about four weeks into the semester and a University course evaluation at the end of the semester.

courses.description:
This course enables students to assemble and analyse research materials collected during their year of study in Japan or their third year of study when they take the local study scheme. Advisory: For Japanese Studies Majors in their final year of attendance.

The Field-Research Based Project, a requirement for all JAS major students, is completed over the course of four years. Preparation for the project begins in year one and year two when students take JAS1010, JAS1050, and JAS1090. In these courses, students gain familiarity with contemporary Japanese politics, economy, culture and society. Students also learn to identify and evaluate academic arguments, locate academic sources, cite sources properly, and identify and assess basic social science and humanities research methods. In the third-year of study, students decide on their research topic and methods, and appropriately apply research methods to collect data for the project. Students who are in the fourth year of study, should have completed data collection and should be writing the paper. Students should have made efforts to speak, read, and write Japanese to achieve their research aims. Students are expected to have used Japanese for activities such as requesting assistance, conducting interviews, creating questionnaires, surveying media sources, and conducting literature reviews. The final-year project completed during the required courses JAS4601 and JAS4602 is thus the final product of the previous three years’ of preparation in both Japanese studies and Japanese language.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Students complete their research paper based on the data they collected during their year in Japan, and using the skills and knowledge attained during year one. Students are expected to (a) become knowledgeable about and critically analyze a specific topic in Japanese studies, (b) master the skills of researching and writing a research paper in English, and (c) gain proficiency in communicating the results of data collection and analysis.

Skills learned through the final-year project are transferable to their future workplaces and graduate school work. In job interviews, job applications, on their resumes, and in graduate school applications, JAS graduates who have successfully completed the project may be able to state the following:

1. They are capable of planning, developing, and implementing a long-term (two-year) project.
2. They are capable of approaching people to make new contacts and creating networks.
3. They are capable of collecting experts’ research results written in Japanese, Chinese, and English. They are capable of analyzing this material, making reasoned conclusions, and clearly communicating the results of your analysis orally and in writing.
4. They are capable of collecting original data, analyzing, and communicating the results of their analysis.
courses.course_syllabus: Students will submit a mid-year draft of the paper, approximately 15 to 25 pages. The final paper, approximately 30 to 40 pages will be sumitted before the end of the semester. In addition, students will make an oral presentation on a chosen date.
courses.feedback_evaluation: One month mid-term survey and course evaluation
courses.required_readings: It is a final-year project. No required reading list is available.
courses.recommended_readings: It is a final-year project. No recommended reading list is available.

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Theory and Research Methods
courses.description: This core course is designed to prepare students to conduct postgraduate research in Japanese Studies. It introduces key conceptual and methodological approaches from different disciplines. Students are encouraged to critically evaluate various approaches and consider how they may be integrated for their particular research projects. The course teaches the analytical skills and methodological tools that will allow students to develop and execute a research project. These skills include research design, data collection, analysis and developing writing strategies.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Articulated M.Phil-Ph.D in Japanese Studies
courses.learning_outcomes:
At the conclusion of the course, students will be able to:
1) identify and analyze the main methodological and theoretical approaches adopted in the study of Japanese society and culture in recent decades.
2) articulate the basic trends in the social development of modern Japan.
courses.course_syllabus:
The course syllabus is tentative depending on the semester and the research needs of individual students taking this course.
Week 1: Introduction to the Course
Week 2: Research Theories & Methods
Week 3: History & Religion
Reading:
• Bellah, Robert. 1957(1985). Tokugawa Religion: The Cultural Roots of Modern Japan. New York: MacMillan Press.
Week 4: Modernization
Reading:
• Vogel, Ezra. 1963. Japan’s New Middle Class: the Salary Man and his Family in a Tokyo Suburb. Berkeley, CA: University of California Press.
Week 5: Cultural Practices / The Self
Reading:
• Lebra, Takie Sugiyama. (1976). Japanese Patterns of Behavior. East-West Center.
Week 6: Philosophy / Society
Reading:
• Watsuji, Tetsuro. 1935(1962). Climate and Culture. (Various publishers)
Week 7: Post-Structuralism/Post-Modernism
Reading:
• Kondo, Dorinne. 1990. Crafting Selves: Power, Gender, and Discourses of Identity in a Japanese Workplace. Chicago: University of Chicago Press.
Week 8: Technology & Society
Reading:
• Sone, Yuji. (2017). Japanese Robot Culture: Performance, Imagination, and Modernity. Palgrave MacMillan.
3
Week 9: Sociology & Social Constructionism
Reading: (Co-Presentation)
• Goodman, Roger, Imoto, Y., & Toivonen, T. H. I. (Eds.). 2012. A sociology of Japanese youth: From returnees to NEETs (Vol. 83). Routledge.
Week 10: Language & Society
Reading:
• Inoue, Miyako. (2006). Vicarious Language Gender and Linguistic Modernity in Japan. UC Press.
Week 11: Gender & Society
Reading: 2 Presentations
 Robertson, Jennifer. (1998). Takarazuka: sexual politics and popular culture in modern Japan. Berkeley: University of California Press.
 Ho, Swee-Lin. (2018). Friendship and Work Culture of Women Managers in Japan Tokyo After Ten. London and New York: Routledge.
Week 12: Disaster & Applied Anthropology
Reading:
• Takezawa, Shoichiro. 2016. The Aftermath of the 2011 East Japan Earthquake and Tsunami: Living among the Rubble. Lexington Books.
Week 13: Final Reflections Due

1) Class Participation (online discussion) 20%
2) Book Reviews (4 in total) 50%
3) Online Presentations (2 in total) 30%

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Special Topics in Japanese Studies
courses.description: This course will focus on specific topics in Japanese studies that are not covered in the regular course offering. These topics will reflect academic needs/interests and faculty expertise.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Articulated M.Phil-Ph.D in Japanese Studies or MA in Japanese Studies
courses.learning_outcomes: Upon completion of the course, students are expected to achieve the learning goals stated by the instructor who will be an expert in the course topic.
courses.course_syllabus: The course covers an array of topics on Japanese society, history, culture and other fields of interests in accord with teacher's reseach expertise. Class lectures include but not limited to discussions, presentations, reports on readings/video materials, Through these, students are expected to develop an in-depth understanding of a particular study topic from a global perspective.
courses.feedback_evaluation: Students’ feedback will be collected through the course evaluation, through a JAS mid-term survey, and through informal discussion with the teacher.
courses.required_readings: Subject to course outlines/information updates at lectures
courses.recommended_readings: Subject to course outlines/information updates at lectures

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Teaching Japanese as a Second Language
courses.description: This course introduces and discusses various concepts in teaching Japanese as a second language which include course design, the procedures of handling classroom activities, the proper usage of teaching materials and teaching tools, method of assessment of students, etc. These discussions are essential to students who will pursue their career goals in Japanese language teaching upon graduation. Moreover, the course will also examine methods used in teaching foreign language with a focus on Japanese language education.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Articulated M.Phil-Ph.D in Japanese Studies or MA in Japanese Studies
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Students should acquire the technique of course design and teaching plan preparation.
2. Students should be able to apply various methods to teach the four essential skills in language acquisition, namely, reading, writing, listening and speaking.
3. Students should be able to explain those significant grammatical points in an easy-to-understand way.
4. Students should acquire the technique of classroom management.
5. Students should be able to apply the cultural elements to assist foreign language teaching.

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Tradition and Modernity in Contemporary Japan
courses.description: This course aim at analyzing the relationship between traditional culture and modern society in Japan. It explores key cultural and social concepts in historical contexts, and considers how ideas and institutions have changed over time.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Articulated M.Phil-Ph.D in Japanese Studies or MA in Japanese Studies
courses.learning_outcomes:
1) Students are expected to gain an understanding of the relationship between historical social-cultural institutions and modern formations of Japanese life including work, family, gender roles, marriage, leisure, and religion.

2) Students will be able to identify and critically assess different ways in which traditional culture is interpreted in modern Japan.

2) Students will be able to critically assess different approaches to understanding Japanese society and culture.

3) Students will have improved their academic essay writing and research skills.
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1: Introduction: Images of Japan

Week 2: Perspectives on Tradition and Modernity

Week 3: Interpretations of Japanese Traditions

Week 4: Education

Week 5: Work and Workplaces

Week 6: Home and Family

Week 7: Gendered Experience

Week 8: Power, Politics, and Media

Week 9: Leisure and Popular Culture

Week 10: Religion

Week 11: Japan in Global Perspective

Week 12: Student Presentations

Week 13: Conclusions
courses.feedback_evaluation:
- One-month survey

- Informal discussion with students

- Course evaluation

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Advanced English-Japanese Translation
courses.description:
This course introduces some of the key concepts in translation theory and focuses on their application to English to Japanese translation practice. The course provides students who have a solid knowledge of basic Japanese with a practical experience of translation and aims to develop their translation skills to a high level of proficiency.

Students will work with a wide range of text types, become familiar with different translation techniques, and develop intellectual perspectives on the activity of translation.

Texts are drawn from various sources including news media, internet, business communication, academic literature, fiction and films. Students are required to pass a placement test or obtain equivalent competence in Japanese before course registration.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
For students of MPhil-PhD Japanese Studies & MA Japanese Studies
Exclusion= JASP4380
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Apply linguistic and sociocultural knowledge to produce translations from English to Japanese.
2. Recognize text types and their conventions, formulate best approaches for translating different types of texts, and reflect on strategies for problem solving.
3. Develop the ability to reflect on the process of translation.
4. Learn to analyze the relationship between translation and culture.
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1: Introduction to translation studies

Week 2: Skills and knowledge necessary to work as a successful translator

Week 3: Translation strategies, methods, approaches, and different degrees of freedom (literal, free, semantic, communicative,

or adaptation)

Week 4: Techniques for translating different text types (literary, academic, business, and media)

Week 5: Techniques commonly adopted in English – Japanese translation (omission, expansion, and changing parts of text)

Week 6: Difficulties specific to English – Japanese translation (rhetorical devices, proverbs, and idioms)

Week 7: Methods for translating culture-specific items (transcription, naturalization, localization, and cultural equivalent)

Week 8: Translation and culture, interlingual translation and intralingual translation

Week 9: Computer-aided human translation and machine translation

Week 10: Oral dimension, consecutive interpreting and simultaneous interpreting

Week 11: Group presentation

Week 12: Group presentation

Week 13: Review and conclusions
courses.feedback_evaluation:
Assignment: Students will be assigned one piece of homework (500 words) to practice their translation skills.

• Translation project: Each student will produce a translation of a set text of 1000 words accompanied by a 400-word reflective commentary outlining the student's selfreflections on the strategies and procedures that s/he has employed for this translation project. The commentary may also address such questions as: How is your experience of doing this project related to what you have learned about translation during this course? In what way(s) has this project been beneficial for you? How might it be beneficial in the future, both for you and for others?

• Presentation: Students will work in groups of 3-4 members on a translation exercise relating to a topic of their choosing. Each group will translate an English text of 2000words into Japanese and present to the whole class a summary of the work they have undertaken in the process of planning the project, researching the topic and translating the text.

In general, the summary should introduce the text (What is the text you chose to translate? Why did you choose this text? What do you particularly like about this text? What does your reader need to know about the author of the text? What does your reader need to know about the context from which the source text emerged?, etc.), and address the group's choice of translation approach(es) and difficulties encountered in the exercise in both general terms and specific to English – Japanese translation (What are the conventions of the chosen text type? What similar texts did you use as your reference? What difficulties did you anticipate in the translation? What theoretical approach to translation did you follow? What translation strategies and techniques did you adopt and why? Did you encounter cultural problems in your translation? What process did you follow in preparing the translation? How did you solve the anticipated problems? Are you satisfied with your solutions? Where are you dissatisfied? Who or what sources helped you? How did you organize your work as a group?, etc.).

• Active participation: Assessed on each student's engagement in class discussions,

groupwork and practical translation tasks in-class.
