courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Theory and Methods in Japanese Studies
courses.description: This course is intended to introduce and broaden the understanding of contemporary Japanese society from the modern period to the contemporary period. By introducing some of the theories and methodological approaches that have been used to examine Japanese society, students will learn, and critically discuss perspectives of history, sociology, anthropology and cultural studies and media analyses. Moreover, utilizing a variety of readings, lectures, and film, students will engage in a multi-level analysis of contemporary Japan.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Articulated M.Phil-Ph.D in Japanese Studies or MA in Japanese Studies
courses.learning_outcomes: The course has a number of major learning outcomes. (1) Students are expected to critically discuss Japanese society using a variety of theories and methodological approaches. (2) Students are expected to be able to clarify and articulate thoughtful and well-founded opinions from a comparative perspectives. (3) Students are expected to develop the ability to engage critically in academic discussions through weekly contributions to in-class discussions and presentations. (4) Students are expected to develop their research skills through a short-term research project that includes a written assignment and in-class presentation.

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Critical Analysis of Popular Culture
courses.description: This course aims to provide students with an in-depth understanding of the development of Japanese popular culture. It discusses major theories, approaches and writings on Japanese popular culture related to globalization, fandom, media studies, and gender studies. Through the class discussion and presentation, student will be able to critically appraise and analyze popular culture in Japan.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Articulated M.Phil-Ph.D in Japanese Studies or MA in Japanese Studies
courses.learning_outcomes:
- Articulate the basic theoretical concepts of popular culture.

- Appraise the ideologies, practices and trends of popular culture in Japan.

- Analyze and think critically about the issues in Japanese popular culture.

- Understand the history and diversity of Japanese popular culture creations.

- Assess the impacts of popular culture in Japan and understand major issues and debates related to popular culture in Japan internally as well as its relation to the world
courses.course_syllabus:
Topic:

Japanese Popular Culture

Contents/fundamental concepts:

Japanese Popular Culture, Soft Power, Encoding and Decoding Model, Participatory Theory, Globalization, Emotional Consumption, Cultural Imperialism

Course schedule:

Lesson 1

What is Japanese Popular Culture?

Discussion: What can one learn about Japanese society from its popular culture?

Lesson 2

Japanese Animation and Socialization

Required Readings:

Izawa, Eri (2000) “The Romantic, Passionate Japanese in Anime: A Look at the Hidden Japanese Soul” In Japan pop! : Inside the world of Japanese popular culture. N.Y.: M.E. Sharpe. Pp.138-153.

Lee, William (2000). “From Sazae-san to Crayon Shin-chan: Family Anime, Social Change, and Nostalgia in Japan” In Japan pop! : Inside the world of Japanese popular culture . N.Y.: M.E. Sharpe. Pp.186-206.

Optional Readings:

Melek Ortabasi (2000) “Fictional Fantasy or Historical Fact? The Search for Japanese Identity in Miyazaki Hayao's Mononokehime” In A century of popular culture in Japan. Lewiston: Edwin Mellen Press. Pp. 199-228.

Mackie, Vera (2008). How to Be a Girl: Mainstream Media Portrayals of Transgendered Lives in Japan In Asian Studies Review: Sep 2008:32:3. Pp. 411-423.

Discussion: Do you agree “Gender stereotype in Japanese animation is a social phenomenon” in Japanese popular culture?

Lesson 3

Japanese Film and Japaneseness

Required Reading:

Desser, David (2000) “From Enka to Anime: Popular Japanese Cinema, 1945-1990” In A century of popular culture in Japan. Lewiston: Edwin Mellen Press. Pp. 79-100.

Anisfield, Nancy (1995). “Godzilla/Gojiro: Evolution of the nuclear metaphor” In Journal of Popular Culture: Winter 1995:29:3. Pp.53-62.

Optional Reading:

Graddy Hendrix (2001). “From Nuclear Nightmare to Networked Nirvana: Futuristic Utopianism in Japanese SF Films of the 2000s” In World Literature Today: Mar-Jun 2010. Pp.55-57.

Ryfle, Steve (2005). Godzilla’s Footprint In The Virginia Quarterly Review: Winter 2005: 81:1. Pp.44-63.

Discussion: Do you agree Godzilla is representing America?

Lesson 4

Robot Culture and Technological development in Japan

Required Readings:

Schilling, M. (2003). Doraemon: Making Dreams come true IN Japan Quarterly: Oct 1993:40:4. Pp.405-417.

Kitano, Naho (2006). “Rinri’: An Incitement towards the Existence of Robots in Japanese Society” In International Review of Information Ethics Vol. 6 (12/2006), Pp. 79-83.

http://www.i-r-i-e.net/inhalt/006/006\_Kitano.pdf

Optional Readings:

Teresko, J. (2008). “The future is Now for the Robot Revolution: The next wave of robots will be remarkably human in appearance and function” In Factory Automation, Japan 2008. Pp.18-22.

The Daily Yomiuri (2006). “Silicon smiles: Robots part of Japan’s culture” In The Daily Yomiuri (Tokyo) 10 June 2006. Pp.1.

Discussion: How Karakuri Ningyo carries on its tradition in modern Japanese innovations and technological development?

Lesson 5

Japan Hostess and Non verbal communication

Required Reading:

A. Allison. (1994). Nightwork: Sexuality, Pleasure, and Corporate Masculinity in a Tokyo Hostess Club. University of Chicago Press. Pp.7-23.

Optional Reading:

Turner, Janice (2004). My life as a Tokyo bar hostess in the Times (London (UK)) 07 May 2004: Pp.6.

Desser, David (1995). Japan nightwork: Sexuality, Pleasure and Coroporate Masculinity in a Tokyo hostess Club In The journal of Asian Studies: Feb 1995:54, 1:213

Suzuki, Mayumi (2007). A Japanese Hostess Club in Hong Kong, Hong Kong: The University of Hong Kong (MPhil Thesis).

http://hub.hku.hk/handle/10722/51699

Discussion: What are the influences bring from “New Geisha” to “Traditional Japanese Culture”?

Lesson 6

New Image of Otaku and Otaku Marketing

Required Reading:

Douglas, McGray and Hiroki, Azuma (2005): “Otaku Unmasked: The Life, Death and Rebirth of Japan’s Pop Culture” In Japan Society (http://www.japansociety.org/content.cfm/otaku\_unmasked)

Takahiro Ueda (2007). “Otaku Culture and Its Discontents:

A Record of Talk Delivered at “The Colloquium in Visual and Cultural Studies”.

http://www.ritsumei.ac.jp/acd/cg/lt/rb/623/623PDF/ueda.pdf

Optional Reading:

Thomas LaMarre, Azuma Hiroki, Yuriko Furuhata and Marc Steinberg (2007). “The Animalization of Otaku Culture” In Mechademia Volume 2, 2007, University of Minnesota Press Pp. 175-187.

Rush, Adam (2009). “Otaku Creations:The Participatory Culture of Fansubbing” In Kinephanos Journal:“Digital Imageries: Culture and Reception”, Fall 2009.

http://www.kinephanos.ca/Revue\_files/Otaku\_v1n1.pdf

Discussion: Do Otaku influencing the society or the society create those Otaku?

Lesson 7

Japanese Idol and Participatory Theory

Required Reading:

Aoyagi Hiroshi (2000) “Pop Idols and the Asian Identity” In Japan pop! : Inside the world of Japanese popular culture. N.Y.: M.E. Sharpe. Pp.309-326. (Blackboard)

Optional Reading:

Belson, Ken and Bremner, Brian (2004). “Kitty Goes Abroad” In Hello Kitty: The Remarkable Story of Sanrio and the Billion Dollar Feline Phenomenon. Singapore: John Wiley & Sons (Asia) Pte Ltd. Pp.87-116.

Discussion: What are the pros and cons of Japanese format Selling? Do you think it can be widely accept in other countries?

Lesson 8

Japan Music and Emotional Consumption

Required Reading:

Shimatachi Hiro R.(2000) A Karaoke Perspective on International Relations, In Japan pop! : Inside the world of Japanese popular culture. N.Y.: M.E. Sharpe. Pp.101-108.

Yano, Christine R. (2000) “The Marketing of Tears: Consuming Emotions in Japanese Popular Song” In Japan pop! : Inside the world of Japanese popular culture. N.Y.: M.E. Sharpe. Pp.60-74.

Optional Reading:

Banks, John A.L. (2003). Negotiating Participatory Culture in the New Media Environment: Auran and the Traniz Online Community – An (Im)possible Relation In MelbourneDAC2003 (http://hypertext.rmit.edu.au/dac/papers/Banks.pdf)

Fabienne, D. W. (2004). Virtually Multicultural: Trans-Asian Identity and Gender in an international fan community of a Japanese star In New Media & Society, London 16(4): Pp.507-528.

Discussion:

Do you agree emotional consumption is one of the key factors lead to the success of Japanese popular culture industry?

Lesson 9

Japan TV Drama and Cultural Globalization

Required Reading:

Lee Dong-Hoo (2004). “Cultural contact with Japanese TV dramas: modes of reception and narrative transparency” In Feeling Asian modernities: transnational consumption of Japanese TV dramas. Hong Kong: Hong Kong University Press. Pp. 251-274.

Lee Ming-tsung (2004). “Traveling with Japanese TV dramas: cross-cultural orientation and flowing identification of contemporary Taiwanese youth”. In Feeling Asian modernities: transnational consumption of Japanese TV dramas. Hong Kong: Hong Kong University Press. Pp. 129-154.

Optional Reading:

Gossmann Hilaria M. (2000) “New Role Models for Men and Women? Gender in Japanese TV Dramas” In Japan pop! : Inside the world of Japanese popular culture. N.Y.: M.E. Sharpe. Pp.207-221.

Iwabuchi, Koichi (2002). “Becoming Culturally Proximate: Japanese TV Dramas in Taiwan”. In Recentering Globalization: Popular Culture and Japanese Transnationalsim. U.S.: Duke University Press. Pp.121-157.

Ko, Yu Fen (2004). “The desired form : Japanese idol dramas in Taiwan” In Feeling Asian modernities : transnational consumption of Japanese TV dramas. Hong Kong: Hong Kong University Press. Pp. 107-128.

Discussion: Why Japanese TV drama is so popular in Asia instead of other part of the world?

Lesson 10

Japanese Manga and its Influence to HK

Required Reading:

MacWilliams Mark Wheeler (2000).”Japanese Comic Books and Religion: Osamu Tezuka's Story of the Buddha” In Japan pop! : inside the world of Japanese popular culture . N.Y. : M.E. Sharpe. Pp.109-137.

Tsurumi Maia (2000) Gender Roles and Girls' Comics in Japan: The Girls and Guys of Yukan Club. ” In Japan pop!: Inside the world of Japanese popular culture . N.Y.: M.E. Sharpe. Pp.171-185.

Optional Reading:

Wong, Wendy siuyi (2006). Globalization Manga: From Japan to Hong Kong and Beyond In Mechademia Volume 1, 2006. University of Minnesota Press. Pp.23-45.

Wong, Wendy siuyi (2008). From Transnationalization to Globalization. The Experience of Hong Kong. In Mechademia Volume 3, 2008. University of Minnesota Press. Pp.226-268.

Discussion: Do you think Japanese popular culture is still influencing Hong Kong societies?

Lesson 11

Popular Culture and Education

Required Reading:

Levi, Toni (2008). “Anime and Manga: It’s not all Make-Believe”.

http://aboutjapan.japansociety.org/content.cfm/anime\_and\_manga\_its\_not\_all\_make-believe

Condry, Ian (2009). “Teaching Anime: Exploring a Transnational and Transmedia Movement”. http://aboutjapan.japansociety.org/content.cfm/teaching\_anime\_\_exploring\_a\_transnational\_and\_transmedia\_movemen

Optional Reading:

Spindler, William Jay. “Anime and Manga, Japanese foreign language students, and the assumption popular culture has a place in the classroom.” University of Calfornia: Digital dissertation consortium.

Discussion: Do you agree bringing popular culture into classroom?

Lesson 12

Popular Culture in Global Context

Required Reading:

Shiraishi Saya S.(2000) “Doraemon Goes Abroad” In Japan pop! : Inside the world of Japanese popular culture. N.Y.: M.E. Sharpe. Pp.287-308.

Iwabuchi, Koichi (2002). “Japan’s Asian Dreamworld” In Recentering Globalization: Popular Culture and Japanese Transnationalsim. U.S.: Duke University Press. Pp.199-210.

Optional Reading:

Anne Allison (2000) “Popular Culture Go Global?: How Japanese "Scouts" and "Rangers" Fare in the US” In A century of popular culture in Japan. Lewiston: Edwin Mellen Press. Pp. 127-154.

Discussion: What are the opportunities and obstacles (both internal and external) for Japan on being a popular culture empire in 21st century?

Lesson 13

In Class Quiz

Through the class discussion and presentation, student will be able to critically appraise and analyze Japanese film.

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: The Global Japanese
courses.description: This course examines Japanese migrants and communities in diverse locations such as East Asia, Oceania, Europe, and Americas. Through tracing historical experiences of Japanese emigrants (Nikkeijin) and exploring their transnational cultural practices, students will develop a picture of Japan’s changing roles in a globalized world from an aggressive colonizer to an economic superpower to a shrinking society. Topics may include the variegated roles of Japanese migrants in settler communities and colonial societies, gendered communities of practice, transnational networks and communication, linguistic and culinary hybridity, and Japanese migrants’ contribution to the global spread and dissemination of Japanese popular culture
courses.enrollment_requirement: PhD in Japanese Studies, Mphil in Japanese Studies, MA in Japanese Studies.
courses.course_syllabus:
| **Week** | **Content** | **Note** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | **Historical memory and cultural construction: Introduction to the concept of diaspora and the processes of migration, integration, and intermarriage** | Add/Drop |
| 2 | **Globalizing Japanese crafts: Chefs, bartenders, hair stylists, artists, and athletes** | Add/Drop |
| 3 | **Transnational movements before and during Japan’s national seclusion** | |
| | Chinese New Year | No class |
| 4 | **Modern emigration policy and Nikkei communities in Hawaii, North America, and Latin America** | |
| 5 | **Imperial expansionism and colonial settlements in East and Southeast Asia** | |
| 6 | **Nikkei cuisine and Japanised Western dishes** | |
| 7 | **The transnational flow of ideas: J-pop, soft power and ‘cool Japan’ campaign** Individual consultation for Final Essay | Consultation for Group presentations |
| 8 | **Humanoid Robots, Animated Characters and National Religion of Japan** Individual consultation for Final Essay | Consultation for Group presentations |
| 9 | **The legacy of Japanese-style management** Individual consultation for Final Essay | Consultation for Group presentations |
| 10 | **Japan’s digital diffusion: Mediation-type platforms, convergence culture and** **derivative work** Individual consultation for Final Essay | |
| 11 | **Review and discussion** Individual consultation for Final Essay | **Group presentations start** |
| 12 | **Review and discussion** Individual consultation for Final Essay | **Group presentations** |
| | Easter Holidays | No class |
| 13 | **Review and discussion** Individual consultation for Final Essay | **Group presentations end** |
| | | **Final Essay submission** |
courses.feedback_evaluation: Students will be asked to fill out a mid-term survey about one month into the course, and a university course evaluation at the end of the semester. In addition, students are welcome to discuss their questions or concerns about the course with the instructor at any time during the semester.

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Japanese Philosophy
courses.description:
This course aims to understand and analyze Japanese philosophy, which is an important topic for reflecting on the roots of Japanese intellectual history and contemporary philosophical debates.

Three areas will be discussed: 1) Japanese intellectual traditions such as those found in Buddhism, Confucianism and Shinto/Native Studies thought; 2) modern Japanese philosophers such as Nishida Kitaro, Kuki Shuzo and Watsuji Tetsuro; and 3) contemporary Japanese thought: Karatani Kojin, Post-311 philosophy, etc.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
For students of MPhil-PhD Japanese Studies & MA Japanese Studies
Exclusion= MA students cannot be enrolled in both JASP5625 and JASP5619 within their study period in the Programme
courses.learning_outcomes: Upon completion of the course, students are expected to understand basic concepts of Japanese philosophy, and their influence on traditional, modern and contemporary Japanese culture. They can critically read texts written by Japanese philosophers, and analyze Japanese cultural phenomena from a philosophical perspective.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction

2. Traditional Japanese thought: Buddhism, Confucianism, Shinto and Native Studies

3. Modern Japanese philosophy: Nishida Kitaro, Kuki Shuzo, Watsuji Tetsuro

4. Contemporary Japanese thought: Karatani Kojin, Post-311 philosophy

5. Conclusion
courses.feedback_evaluation: Mid-term survey, and Course evaluation.
courses.required_readings: Heisig, J., Kasulis, T. and Maraldo, J. (eds)., 2011. Japanese Philosophy: A Sourcebook. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press.

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Independent Research Project

meetings.instructor:
Professor Jeremy Avrum YELLEN, 
Professor Lynne Yukie NAKANO

courses.description: This course enables MA students to independently investigate a research topic with the assistance of a supervising professor. The student is expected to propose the topic herself/himself and may proceed if the topic is approved by a suitable supervisor. The topic may be in any field that is related to Japanese Studies if the supervisor’s consent is obtained. The research question, research methods, and length of the paper should be decided at the start of the course through discussion and agreement between the student and the supervisor. The number and frequency of meetings between the student and the supervisor should be decided at the start of the course after discussion and agreement between the student and the supervisor.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Japanese Studies
courses.learning_outcomes:
At the conclusion of this course, students will have:

1. produced an original research paper.

2. mastered skills of conducting and writing a literature review and analyzing data.

3. developed their knowledge in a specific area of Japanese studies based on their interest.
courses.course_syllabus:
| **Topic** | **Contents/fundamental concepts** |
| --- | --- |
| 1. Select research topic 2. Conduct literature review 3. Data collection and analysis 4. Independent work | Students are expected to select a topic with the guidance of his/her supervisor. Students are expected to take the initiative to conduct and write a literature review. Students will collect original data if appropriate or analyze existing data in an original way. Students will work independently and will take the initiative to meet with their supervisors regularly to facilitate the writing project. |
courses.feedback_evaluation: Students are welcome to speak to the instructor about their views of the course. In addition, students will be asked to fill out a mid-term survey after about four weeks into the semester and a University course evaluation at the end of the semester.
courses.required_readings:
Readings will depend on the research topic chosen, and will be decided upon by the teacher and the

student. The student should conduct a literautre review to select appropriate readings, with the

guidance of the supervisor.

courses.subject: JASP
courses.title: Thesis Research
courses.description: In this course, a student is required to meet with his/her supervisor regularly who provides necessary guidance and supervision to write up a thesis and monitors the student's academic progress.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in the Articulated M.Phil-Ph.D Programme in Japanese Studies
courses.learning_outcomes: Students are required to complete a research report under guidance of his/her supervisor.
courses.course_syllabus: Students are required to meet/communicate with their supervisors regularly and report on their research progress as requested by individual supervisors. The course syllabus is subject to change depending on the semester and the research needs of individual students taking this course. Please refer to course syllabus distributed in class.
courses.feedback_evaluation: Informal feedback
courses.required_readings: Readings are assigned according to students’ academic needs, research interests and faculty expertise. Please refer to course syllabus distributed in class.
courses.recommended_readings: Readings are assigned according to students’ academic needs, research interests and faculty expertise. Please refer to course syllabus distributed in class.
